starter_code.sql:
-- Write your SQL query here
-- Calculate the percentage of transactions that were disputed for each merchant category

reference_solution.sql:
SELECT m.category,
       COUNT(t.txn_id) AS total_txns,
       COUNT(d.dispute_id) AS disputed_txns,
       ROUND(100.0 * COUNT(d.dispute_id) / COUNT(t.txn_id), 2) AS dispute_rate_pct
FROM merchants m
JOIN transactions t ON m.merchant_id = t.merchant_id
LEFT JOIN disputes d ON t.txn_id = d.txn_id
GROUP BY m.category
ORDER BY dispute_rate_pct DESC;

test_cases.label: Dispute rate by category with correct percentage calculation
Categories ranked by dispute rate descending
Categories with no disputes have 0.00% dispute rate

metadata.reference_approach: Join merchants to transactions to get all transactions per category, then LEFT JOIN disputes to identify which transactions have associated disputes. Use COUNT(d.dispute_id) on the left-joined nullable column to count only transactions with disputes, divide by COUNT(t.txn_id) to get the total transaction count per category, multiply by 100, round to 2 decimals, and group by category. Order by dispute rate descending to highlight the highest-risk categories.

starter_code.sql:
-- Write your SQL query here
SELECT
  m.name,
  m.category,
  COUNT(t.txn_id) AS txn_count,
  SUM(t.amount) AS total_volume
FROM merchants m
JOIN transactions t ON m.merchant_id = t.merchant_id
WHERE t.status = 'completed'
  AND t.created_at >= '2024-01-01' AND t.created_at < '2025-01-01'
GROUP BY m.merchant_id, m.name, m.category
ORDER BY total_volume DESC
LIMIT 5;

starter_code.sql:
-- Write your SQL query here
-- Find customers who transacted with 3 or more different merchants in 2024

reference_solution.sql:
SELECT customer_id, COUNT(DISTINCT merchant_id) AS merchant_count
FROM transactions
WHERE status = 'completed'
  AND created_at >= '2024-01-01' AND created_at < '2025-01-01'
GROUP BY customer_id
HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT merchant_id) >= 3
ORDER BY merchant_count DESC;

metadata.reference_approach: Use COUNT(DISTINCT merchant_id) within a GROUP BY customer_id to count how many unique merchants each customer interacted with. Filter transactions to only 'completed' status and those within the 2024 calendar year using timestamp range checks. Apply HAVING clause to retain only customer groups with 3 or more distinct merchants. Order by merchant count descending to surface the most active multi-merchant customers. This pattern isolates the 'customer cohort' query efficiently without needing self-joins or complex subqueries.

starter_code.sql:
-- Write your SQL query here
-- Calculate the percentage of viewers who watched more than 80% of each show

reference_solution.sql:
SELECT s.title,
       COUNT(*) AS total_views,
       SUM(CASE WHEN v.watch_pct >= 80 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS completed_views,
       ROUND(100.0 * SUM(CASE WHEN v.watch_pct >= 80 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) / COUNT(*), 2) AS completion_rate_pct
FROM views v
JOIN shows s ON v.show_id = s.show_id
GROUP BY s.show_id, s.title
ORDER BY completion_rate_pct DESC;

test_cases.label: Completion rate by show (all shows ranked by completion)
Shows with high completion rates appear first
Edge case: Shows with mixed completion rates (some <80%, some >=80%)

metadata.reference_approach: Join the views table with the shows table to match each view record to its corresponding show. Group results by show_id and title. For each group, calculate the total number of views, count how many views exceeded the 80% watch threshold using a CASE WHEN conditional aggregate, and compute the completion rate percentage by dividing completed views by total views and multiplying by 100. Round to 2 decimal places and order by completion rate in descending order to highlight shows with the highest viewer completion.

starter_code.sql:
-- Write your SQL query here
-- Find the top 3 most watched shows per genre by total unique viewers
-- Use DENSE_RANK() to handle ties properly

reference_solution.sql:
WITH ranked AS (
  SELECT s.genre, s.title,
         COUNT(DISTINCT v.user_id) AS unique_viewers,
         DENSE_RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY s.genre ORDER BY COUNT(DISTINCT v.user_id) DESC) AS rnk
  FROM views v JOIN shows s ON v.show_id = s.show_id
  GROUP BY s.genre, s.show_id, s.title
)
SELECT genre, title, unique_viewers, rnk
FROM ranked WHERE rnk <= 3
ORDER BY genre, rnk;

test_cases.label: Top 3 shows per genre by unique viewers
DENSE_RANK handles ties correctly (no rank gaps)
Edge case: multiple genres with varying viewer counts

metadata.reference_approach: Use a CTE with a window function to partition views by genre and rank shows within each genre by count of distinct viewers in descending order. DENSE_RANK() ensures that if two shows tie for 2nd place, both receive rank 2 and the next show gets rank 3 (not rank 4), which is crucial for business logic that requires exactly 'top 3' including ties. Filter to rnk <= 3 and order by genre and rank for clarity.

CREATE TABLE users (

starter_code.sql:
-- Write your SQL query here
-- Find users who listened to 5 or more different genres this week

reference_solution.sql:
SELECT s.user_id, COUNT(DISTINCT t.genre) AS genres_listened
FROM streams s
JOIN tracks t ON s.track_id = t.track_id
WHERE s.streamed_at >= datetime('now', '-7 days')
GROUP BY s.user_id
HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT t.genre) >= 5
ORDER BY genres_listened DESC;

test_cases.label: Multi-genre listeners with 5+ genres in last 7 days
Correct genre count aggregation and filtering
Users below threshold excluded; ordering by genre diversity

metadata.reference_approach: Join the streams table to tracks on track_id to access genre information. Filter streams to only those from the last 7 days using a datetime comparison. Group by user_id and count the distinct genres they listened to. Use HAVING to retain only users with 5 or more distinct genres. Order the results by genre count in descending order to show the most diverse listeners first. This query identifies power users with broad taste across multiple genres, useful for recommendation targeting and playlist curation.

title: Spotify: Skip Rate Analysis
problem_statement_markdown: What percentage of streams were skipped before 30 seconds for each genre?

starter_code.sql:
-- Write your SQL query here
-- Calculate the percentage of streams skipped before 30 seconds for each genre

reference_solution.sql:
SELECT t.genre,
       COUNT(*) AS total_streams,
       SUM(CASE WHEN s.skipped = 1 AND s.listen_duration_sec < 30 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS early_skips,
       ROUND(100.0 * SUM(CASE WHEN s.skipped = 1 AND s.listen_duration_sec < 30 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) / COUNT(*), 2) AS skip_rate_pct
FROM streams s
JOIN tracks t ON s.track_id = t.track_id
GROUP BY t.genre
ORDER BY skip_rate_pct DESC;

test_cases.label: Early skip rate by genre (Pop vs Hip-Hop)
Verify count aggregation and percentage calculation
Genres sorted by skip rate descending

metadata.reference_approach: Join streams to tracks to access genre information, then group by genre. For each genre, count total streams and use a compound CASE WHEN (skipped = 1 AND listen_duration_sec < 30) to identify early skips. Divide early skips by total stream count and multiply by 100 to get the percentage, rounding to 2 decimal places. Order by skip_rate_pct in descending order to highlight genres with the highest early skip rates.

title: Twitter: Ad CTR by Advertiser
problem_statement_markdown: What is the click-through rate (CTR = clicks/impressions) and cost-per-click (CPC = spend/clicks) for each advertiser in 2024?

sql_schema.setup_script:
CREATE TABLE users (

CREATE TABLE tweets (

  FOREIGN KEY (user_id) REFERENCES users(user_id)
);

CREATE TABLE follows (
  follow_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
  follower_id INTEGER NOT NULL,
  followee_id INTEGER NOT NULL,
  followed_at TEXT,
  FOREIGN KEY (follower_id) REFERENCES users(user_id),
  FOREIGN KEY (followee_id) REFERENCES users(user_id)
);

CREATE TABLE ads (
  ad_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
  advertiser_id INTEGER NOT NULL,
  tweet_id INTEGER NOT NULL,
  spend REAL,
  impressions INTEGER,
  clicks INTEGER,
  date TEXT,
  FOREIGN KEY (tweet_id) REFERENCES tweets(tweet_id)
);

starter_code.sql:
-- Write your SQL query here
-- Calculate CTR (%) and CPC (USD) for each advertiser in 2024

reference_solution.sql:
SELECT advertiser_id,
       SUM(spend) AS total_spend,
       SUM(impressions) AS total_impressions,
       SUM(clicks) AS total_clicks,
       ROUND(100.0 * SUM(clicks) / NULLIF(SUM(impressions), 0), 4) AS ctr_pct,
       ROUND(SUM(spend) / NULLIF(SUM(clicks), 0), 2) AS cpc_usd
FROM ads
WHERE date >= '2024-01-01' AND date < '2025-01-01'
GROUP BY advertiser_id
ORDER BY total_spend DESC;

test_cases.label: CTR and CPC by advertiser for all 2024 campaigns
Verify NULLIF handles zero denominators and precision formatting
Aggregate multiple ads per advertiser and sort by spend descending

metadata.reference_approach: Aggregate all ad metrics (spend, impressions, clicks) per advertiser for the year 2024 using SUM() and GROUP BY. Calculate CTR as 100 × total_clicks / total_impressions and CPC as total_spend / total_clicks. Use NULLIF on both denominators to prevent division-by-zero errors, rounding CTR to 4 decimal places and CPC to 2 decimal places. Sort results by total_spend descending to identify the highest-spending advertisers.

-- Order by retweets in descending order

reference_solution.sql:
SELECT t.tweet_id, u.username, t.retweets, t.likes, t.posted_at
FROM tweets t

The use of datetime('now', '-7 days') ensures the filter is always relative to query execution time rather than hardcoded dates.

starter_code.sql:
-- Write your SQL query here
-- Show the 4-week rolling average engagement rate (likes + comments / views) per content type for 2024
-- Aggregate to weekly sums first, then apply rolling SUM window functions

reference_solution.sql:
WITH weekly AS (
  SELECT content_type,
         DATE(posted_at, 'weekday 0', '-6 days') AS week,
         SUM(likes + comments) AS total_engagement,
         SUM(views) AS total_views
  FROM posts
  WHERE posted_at >= '2024-01-01' AND posted_at < '2025-01-01'
  GROUP BY content_type, DATE(posted_at, 'weekday 0', '-6 days')
)
SELECT content_type,
       week,
       ROUND(100.0 * SUM(total_engagement) OVER (PARTITION BY content_type ORDER BY week ROWS BETWEEN 3 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW)
             / NULLIF(SUM(total_views) OVER (PARTITION BY content_type ORDER BY week ROWS BETWEEN 3 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW), 0), 2) AS rolling_4w_engagement_pct
FROM weekly
ORDER BY content_type, week;

test_cases.label: Rolling 4-week engagement rate for Article in week of 2024-01-01
Rolling 4-week engagement rate for Video across multiple weeks
Verify engagement rate calculation with different view volumes

metadata.reference_approach: First aggregate posts to weekly sums of engagement (likes + comments) and views per content type using DATE truncation. Then apply a 4-row window (3 preceding + current) with SUM over both the engagement and views columns, partitioned by content_type and ordered by week. Divide rolling engagement sum by rolling views sum to get the correctly weighted rolling engagement rate, multiplying by 100 for percentage. This ensures weeks with higher view volumes influence the average appropriately, rather than averaging individual weekly rates which would give equal weight to all weeks.

title: Airbnb: Guests Who Booked Multiple Cities
problem_statement_markdown: Which guests have made completed bookings in 3 or more different cities?

starter_code.sql:
-- Write your SQL query here
-- Find guests who have completed bookings in 3 or more different cities

reference_solution.sql:
SELECT b.guest_id, g.name, COUNT(DISTINCT l.city) AS cities_visited
FROM bookings b
JOIN listings l ON b.listing_id = l.listing_id
JOIN guests g ON b.guest_id = g.guest_id
WHERE b.status = 'completed'
GROUP BY b.guest_id, g.name
HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT l.city) >= 3
ORDER BY cities_visited DESC;

test_cases.label: Guest with 3 completed bookings in different cities
Multiple guests meeting threshold sorted by cities

metadata.reference_approach: Join the bookings table to listings (to access city data) and guests (to access guest names). Filter for only 'completed' status bookings to exclude cancelled or pending transactions. Group by guest_id and name, then use COUNT(DISTINCT city) to count unique cities per guest. The HAVING clause filters groups where the distinct city count is 3 or more. Finally, order by cities_visited in descending order to show the most-traveled guests first. This is a classic aggregation pattern for identifying customers who have engaged with multiple categories or locations.

starter_code.sql:
-- Calculate monthly churn rate for each signup cohort
-- Group by the month users subscribed and measure churn within 90 days from their own subscription date
SELECT
  -- Extract the cohort month from subscription date
  -- Count total subscribers in each cohort
  -- Count users who churned within 90 days of their subscription
  -- Calculate churn percentage
FROM users
GROUP BY
ORDER BY

reference_solution.sql:
SELECT
  strftime('%Y-%m', subscribed_at) AS cohort_month,
  COUNT(*) AS total_subscribers,
  SUM(CASE WHEN churned_at IS NOT NULL
           AND julianday(churned_at) <= julianday(subscribed_at) + 90
      THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS churned_within_90d,
  ROUND(100.0 * SUM(CASE WHEN churned_at IS NOT NULL
                          AND julianday(churned_at) <= julianday(subscribed_at) + 90
                     THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) / COUNT(*), 2) AS churn_rate_pct
FROM users
GROUP BY strftime('%Y-%m', subscribed_at)
ORDER BY cohort_month;

test_cases.label: March 2024 cohort with high churn edge case

metadata.reference_approach: This solution implements cohort analysis by grouping users by their subscription month using date truncation. For each cohort, it counts the total subscribers and identifies those who churned within 90 days by comparing their churned_at date against their subscribed_at date plus 90 days. The churn rate percentage is calculated as (churned within 90d / total subscribers) * 100, rounded to 2 decimal places. The key insight is measuring churn relative to each user's own subscription start date rather than against a fixed calendar date, enabling accurate cohort-level retention metrics.

starter_code.sql:
-- Write your SQL query here
-- Find merchants with refund rate > 5% in 2024
-- Use LEFT JOIN to preserve all transactions and count refunds

reference_solution.sql:
SELECT m.name,
       COUNT(t.txn_id) AS total_txns,
       COUNT(r.refund_id) AS refunded_txns,
       ROUND(100.0 * COUNT(r.refund_id) / COUNT(t.txn_id), 2) AS refund_rate_pct
FROM merchants m
JOIN transactions t ON m.merchant_id = t.merchant_id
  AND t.created_at >= '2024-01-01' AND t.created_at < '2025-01-01'
LEFT JOIN refunds r ON t.txn_id = r.txn_id
GROUP BY m.merchant_id, m.name
HAVING 1.0 * COUNT(r.refund_id) / COUNT(t.txn_id) > 0.05
ORDER BY refund_rate_pct DESC;

test_cases.label: High refund rate merchants above 5% in 2024
Verify refund rate calculation and sorting
Edge case: Merchant with exactly 5% refund rate excluded

metadata.reference_approach: The solution uses a LEFT JOIN between transactions and refunds to preserve all transaction records while capturing refund information. By filtering transactions to 2024 in the JOIN clause and using COUNT(r.refund_id), which skips NULL values for non-refunded transactions, we can calculate the refund rate as a percentage. The HAVING clause filters for merchants exceeding 5% refund rate, and results are sorted by refund rate in descending order to show the worst performers first.

starter_code.sql:
-- Write your SQL query here
-- Calculate month-over-month revenue growth rate per merchant category for 2024

reference_solution.sql:
WITH monthly AS (
  SELECT m.category,
         DATE(strftime('%Y-%m-01', t.created_at)) AS month,
         SUM(t.amount) AS monthly_revenue
  FROM merchants m 
  JOIN transactions t ON m.merchant_id = t.merchant_id
  WHERE t.status = 'completed' 
    AND t.created_at >= '2024-01-01' 
    AND t.created_at < '2025-01-01'
  GROUP BY m.category, DATE(strftime('%Y-%m-01', t.created_at))
)
SELECT category, 
       month, 
       monthly_revenue,
       LAG(monthly_revenue) OVER (PARTITION BY category ORDER BY month) AS prev_month_revenue,
       ROUND(100.0 * (monthly_revenue - LAG(monthly_revenue) OVER (PARTITION BY category ORDER BY month))
             / NULLIF(LAG(monthly_revenue) OVER (PARTITION BY category ORDER BY month), 0), 2) AS mom_growth_pct
FROM monthly
ORDER BY category, month;

test_cases.label: January to February Food & Beverage MoM growth
First month has NULL previous month revenue

metadata.reference_approach: Use a CTE to aggregate completed transactions by merchant category and month for 2024. Then apply a LAG window function partitioned by category and ordered by month to retrieve the previous month's revenue within each category. Calculate the month-over-month growth percentage as (current_month - previous_month) / previous_month * 100. The PARTITION BY clause ensures each category's growth is tracked independently, and the first month of each category will have a NULL previous month value.

title: Uber: Promo Usage Rate
problem_statement_markdown: What percentage of completed rides used a promotional discount?

starter_code.sql:
-- Write your SQL query here
-- Calculate the percentage of completed rides that used a promotional discount
SELECT
  COUNT(*) AS total_rides,
  COUNT(p.promo_id) AS promo_rides,
  ROUND(100.0 * COUNT(p.promo_id) / COUNT(*), 2) AS promo_usage_pct
FROM rides r
LEFT JOIN promotions p ON r.ride_id = p.ride_id
WHERE r.status = 'completed';

reference_solution.sql:
SELECT
  COUNT(*) AS total_rides,
  COUNT(p.promo_id) AS promo_rides,
  ROUND(100.0 * COUNT(p.promo_id) / COUNT(*), 2) AS promo_usage_pct
FROM rides r
LEFT JOIN promotions p ON r.ride_id = p.ride_id
WHERE r.status = 'completed';

test_cases.label: Promo usage rate for completed rides
Edge case: rides with no promotions included in denominator

metadata.reference_approach: Use a LEFT JOIN between rides and promotions to preserve all completed rides, including those without promotions. Count all rows with COUNT(*) for the total, and COUNT(p.promo_id) to count only rows where a promotion was matched (non-NULL promo_id). This approach avoids filtering with WHERE clauses that would incorrectly exclude non-matching rows. The percentage is calculated as (promo_rides / total_rides) * 100, rounded to 2 decimal places.

title: Twitter: Mutual Followers
problem_statement_markdown: Find all pairs of users who follow each other (mutual follows).

starter_code.sql:
-- Write your SQL query here
-- Find all pairs of users who follow each other (mutual follows)
SELECT
  -- your columns here
FROM follows
WHERE
  -- your conditions here
;

reference_solution.sql:
SELECT f1.follower_id AS user_a, f1.followee_id AS user_b
FROM follows f1
JOIN follows f2 ON f1.follower_id = f2.followee_id AND f1.followee_id = f2.follower_id
WHERE f1.follower_id < f1.followee_id;

test_cases.label: No duplicates in results (no reversed pairs)

metadata.reference_approach: To find mutual followers, perform a self-join of the follows table where the first join condition matches when user A follows user B and the second condition matches when user B follows user A (swapped follower/followee). The WHERE clause with follower_id < followee_id ensures each pair appears only once by enforcing a consistent ordering, avoiding duplicate results where (A, B) and (B, A) would both appear.

SELECT

The date filter is applied on the JOIN condition (not in WHERE) so users without recent tweets appear as NULLs. COUNT(DISTINCT u.user_id) counts total registered users, while COUNT(DISTINCT t.user_id) counts only those with at least one recent tweet (NULLs are skipped). Divide active users by total users and multiply by 100 to get the percentage, rounded to 2 decimal places.

title: Spotify: Top 5 Artists per Genre
problem_statement_markdown: Find the top 5 most streamed artists per genre this year.

starter_code.sql:
-- Write your SQL query here
-- Find the top 5 most streamed artists per genre in 2024
-- Use a CTE to aggregate streams by genre and artist, then apply RANK()

reference_solution.sql:
WITH artist_streams AS (
  SELECT t.genre, a.name AS artist_name, COUNT(s.stream_id) AS stream_count,
         RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY t.genre ORDER BY COUNT(s.stream_id) DESC) AS rnk
  FROM streams s
  JOIN tracks t ON s.track_id = t.track_id
  JOIN artists a ON t.artist_id = a.artist_id
  WHERE s.streamed_at >= '2024-01-01'
  GROUP BY t.genre, a.artist_id, a.name
)
SELECT genre, artist_name, stream_count, rnk
FROM artist_streams
WHERE rnk <= 5
ORDER BY genre, rnk;

test_cases.label: Top 5 artists per genre in 2024 - verify ranking within Hip-Hop
All genres represented with correct stream counts aggregated
Verify rank 1 artist per genre has highest stream count

metadata.reference_approach: Aggregate streams by genre and artist using GROUP BY in a CTE, counting the total stream_id occurrences per artist-genre combination. Apply the RANK() window function partitioned by genre and ordered by stream_count descending to rank artists within each genre. Filter the ranked results to keep only the top 5 (rnk <= 5) and sort by genre and rank for readability. This approach ensures aggregation happens before windowing, which is critical for accurate ranking.

title: Repeat Customer Rate
problem_statement_markdown: What percentage of orders came from customers who had ordered before?

starter_code.sql:
-- Calculate the percentage of orders that came from repeat customers
-- Join orders to customers on customer_id AND store_id
-- A repeat order is when order_date > first_order_date
-- Only count completed orders

SELECT
  COUNT(*) AS total_orders,
  SUM(CASE WHEN o.order_date > c.first_order_date THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS repeat_orders,
  ROUND(100.0 * SUM(CASE WHEN o.order_date > c.first_order_date THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) / COUNT(*), 2) AS repeat_order_rate_pct
FROM orders o
JOIN customers c ON o.customer_id = c.customer_id AND o.store_id = c.store_id
WHERE o.status = 'completed';

reference_solution.sql:
SELECT
  COUNT(*) AS total_orders,
  SUM(CASE WHEN o.order_date > c.first_order_date THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS repeat_orders,
  ROUND(100.0 * SUM(CASE WHEN o.order_date > c.first_order_date THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) / COUNT(*), 2) AS repeat_order_rate_pct
FROM orders o
JOIN customers c ON o.customer_id = c.customer_id AND o.store_id = c.store_id
WHERE o.status = 'completed';

test_cases.label: Edge case: customer with single order only

metadata.reference_approach: To solve this problem, join the orders table with the customers table on both customer_id and store_id to match each order with the customer's first order date. Filter to only completed orders using the WHERE clause. Then use a CASE statement to identify repeat orders as those where the order_date exceeds the customer's first_order_date. Calculate the repeat order rate as a percentage by dividing the count of repeat orders by the total count of completed orders, multiplied by 100 and rounded to 2 decimal places.

title: Cumulative Rides per Driver in January
problem_statement_markdown: Show the cumulative rides completed per driver, day by day, in January 2024.

starter_code.sql:
-- Write your SQL query here
-- Show cumulative rides per driver, day by day, in January 2024

reference_solution.sql:
WITH daily AS (
  SELECT driver_id, DATE(pickup_time) AS ride_date, COUNT(*) AS daily_rides
  FROM rides
  WHERE status = 'completed' AND pickup_time >= '2024-01-01' AND pickup_time < '2024-02-01'
  GROUP BY driver_id, DATE(pickup_time)
)
SELECT driver_id, ride_date, daily_rides,
       SUM(daily_rides) OVER (PARTITION BY driver_id ORDER BY ride_date ROWS UNBOUNDED PRECEDING) AS cumulative_rides
FROM daily
ORDER BY driver_id, ride_date;

test_cases.label: Driver 2 cumulative rides with gap dates
Edge case: Driver 3 with only 2 rides same day

metadata.reference_approach: Use a CTE to aggregate completed rides per driver per day for January 2024. Then apply a window function SUM(daily_rides) OVER (PARTITION BY driver_id ORDER BY ride_date ROWS UNBOUNDED PRECEDING) to compute the running total of daily rides for each driver in chronological order. The ROWS UNBOUNDED PRECEDING clause ensures we sum from the first day up to the current day for each driver, creating the cumulative effect.

title: Top 5 Drivers by Total Earnings
problem_statement_markdown: Who are the top 5 drivers by total earnings (fare + tip + incentives) in each city?

starter_code.sql:
-- Write your SQL query here
-- Find the top 5 drivers by total earnings (fare + tip + incentives) in each city
-- Include drivers with no incentives using LEFT JOIN
-- Use RANK() OVER to rank within each city

reference_solution.sql:
WITH earnings AS (
  SELECT r.city, r.driver_id, d.name,
         SUM(r.fare + r.tip) AS ride_earnings,
         COALESCE(SUM(i.amount), 0) AS incentive_earnings
  FROM rides r
  JOIN drivers d ON r.driver_id = d.driver_id
  LEFT JOIN incentives i ON r.driver_id = i.driver_id
    AND strftime('%Y', i.paid_at) = strftime('%Y', r.pickup_time)
  WHERE r.status = 'completed' AND r.pickup_time >= '2024-01-01' AND r.pickup_time < '2025-01-01'
  GROUP BY r.city, r.driver_id, d.name
),
ranked AS (
  SELECT *, (ride_earnings + incentive_earnings) AS total_earnings,
         RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY city ORDER BY (ride_earnings + incentive_earnings) DESC) AS rnk
  FROM earnings
)
SELECT city, name, ride_earnings, incentive_earnings, total_earnings, rnk
FROM ranked WHERE rnk <= 5 ORDER BY city, rnk;

test_cases.label: Top 5 drivers by total earnings in New York
Top 5 drivers by total earnings in Los Angeles
Drivers with no incentives still included

metadata.reference_approach: Use a CTE to aggregate ride earnings (fare + tip) and incentive earnings per driver per city, applying a LEFT JOIN to incentives to preserve drivers without incentives using COALESCE. In a second CTE, calculate total earnings and apply RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY city) to rank drivers within each city. Finally, filter to rank <= 5 and order by city and rank. This ensures drivers with no incentives are included with 0 incentive earnings, and ties are handled correctly with RANK().

title: Over-Budget Campaigns
problem_statement_markdown: What percentage of campaigns have spent more than their allocated budget?

starter_code.sql:
-- Write your SQL query here
-- Calculate the percentage of campaigns that have spent more than their allocated budget
-- Hint: Pre-aggregate ad_performance by campaign_id first, then join to campaigns

reference_solution.sql:
SELECT
  COUNT(*) AS total_campaigns,
  SUM(CASE WHEN ap.total_spend > c.budget THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS over_budget,
  ROUND(100.0 * SUM(CASE WHEN ap.total_spend > c.budget THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) / COUNT(*), 2) AS over_budget_pct
FROM campaigns c
JOIN (SELECT campaign_id, SUM(spend) AS total_spend FROM ad_performance GROUP BY campaign_id) ap
  ON c.campaign_id = ap.campaign_id;

test_cases.label: Verify campaign count and over-budget count
Edge case: campaigns with no ad performance

metadata.reference_approach: Pre-aggregate spend by campaign_id in a subquery to avoid fan-out issues when joining with the many-row ad_performance table. Use CASE WHEN to flag campaigns where total_spend exceeds budget, then calculate the percentage by dividing the count of over-budget campaigns by the total number of campaigns with ad performance data, multiplied by 100 and rounded to 2 decimal places.

title: Top 3 Industries by Ad Spend
problem_statement_markdown: What are the top 3 industries by total ad spend?

starter_code.sql:
-- Write your SQL query here
-- Find the top 3 industries by total ad spend

reference_solution.sql:
SELECT a.industry, SUM(ap.spend) AS total_spend
FROM advertisers a
JOIN campaigns c ON a.advertiser_id = c.advertiser_id
JOIN ad_performance ap ON c.campaign_id = ap.campaign_id
GROUP BY a.industry
ORDER BY total_spend DESC
LIMIT 3;

test_cases.label: Top 3 industries by total ad spend
Finance should rank first with highest spend
Exactly 3 rows returned with correct column names

metadata.reference_approach: Join the advertisers table to campaigns, then join campaigns to ad_performance to access spend data. Group by industry and aggregate the spend using SUM(). Order the results by total spend in descending order to identify the highest-spending industries. Use LIMIT 3 to return only the top 3 industries. This approach is efficient because it performs a simple global sort-and-limit operation rather than using window functions, making it faster for this use case where you need the absolute top N results without per-partition ranking.

title: High-CTR Campaigns
problem_statement_markdown: Which campaigns have a click-through rate (CTR) above 5%?

starter_code.sql:
-- Write your SQL query here
-- Find campaigns with CTR > 5%
-- Remember: CTR = clicks / impressions
-- Hint: Use HAVING to filter aggregated groups

reference_solution.sql:
SELECT c.name AS campaign_name, a.name AS advertiser,
       SUM(ap.impressions) AS total_impressions,
       SUM(ap.clicks) AS total_clicks,
       ROUND(100.0 * SUM(ap.clicks) / NULLIF(SUM(ap.impressions), 0), 2) AS ctr_pct
FROM campaigns c
JOIN advertisers a ON c.advertiser_id = a.advertiser_id
JOIN ad_performance ap ON c.campaign_id = ap.campaign_id
GROUP BY c.campaign_id, c.name, a.name
HAVING 100.0 * SUM(ap.clicks) / NULLIF(SUM(ap.impressions), 0) > 5
ORDER BY ctr_pct DESC;

test_cases.label: Verify correct CTR calculation with aggregation
Edge case: campaign with low CTR excluded

metadata.reference_approach: This query demonstrates the GROUP BY & HAVING pattern for filtering aggregated results. First, join the three tables (campaigns, advertisers, ad_performance) to combine campaign metadata with performance metrics. Then GROUP BY campaign to aggregate impressions and clicks across all ad records for each campaign. Calculate CTR as clicks divided by impressions (multiplied by 100 for percentage). The key insight is using HAVING with the full aggregate expression (not the alias) to filter for campaigns with CTR > 5%. NULLIF prevents division by zero. Finally, order by CTR descending to show the best-performing campaigns first.

title: High-Fare Cities
problem_statement_markdown: Which cities had an average completed ride fare above $25 in 2024?

starter_code.sql:
-- Write your SQL query here
-- Find cities with average completed ride fare above $25 in 2024
-- Remember to filter by status and year first!

reference_solution.sql:
SELECT city, ROUND(AVG(fare), 2) AS avg_fare, COUNT(*) AS ride_count
FROM rides
WHERE status = 'completed' AND pickup_time >= '2024-01-01' AND pickup_time < '2025-01-01'
GROUP BY city
HAVING AVG(fare) > 25
ORDER BY avg_fare DESC;

test_cases.label: New York avg fare above $25 in 2024
Los Angeles and Houston below $25 threshold

metadata.reference_approach: Filter the rides table to include only completed rides from 2024 using WHERE conditions. Group by city to aggregate data at the city level. Use HAVING to filter groups where the average fare exceeds $25. Round the average fare to 2 decimal places and count the number of rides per city. Order the results by average fare in descending order to show the highest-fare cities first. The key insight is filtering by status before aggregation to avoid skewing results with cancelled rides that may have partial fares.

starter_code.sql:
-- Write your SQL query here
-- Calculate week-over-week conversion rate change per industry for 2024
-- Use LAG() partitioned by industry to compare with previous week's rate

reference_solution.sql:
WITH weekly AS (
  SELECT a.industry, 
         DATE(ap.date, 'weekday 0', '-6 days') AS week,
         SUM(ap.conversions) AS convs, 
         SUM(ap.clicks) AS clicks
  FROM ad_performance ap
  JOIN campaigns c ON ap.campaign_id = c.campaign_id
  JOIN advertisers a ON c.advertiser_id = a.advertiser_id
  WHERE ap.date >= '2024-01-01' AND ap.date < '2025-01-01'
  GROUP BY a.industry, DATE(ap.date, 'weekday 0', '-6 days')
),
rates AS (
  SELECT industry, 
         week,
         ROUND(100.0 * convs / NULLIF(clicks, 0), 2) AS conv_rate,
         LAG(ROUND(100.0 * convs / NULLIF(clicks, 0), 2)) OVER (PARTITION BY industry ORDER BY week) AS prev_conv_rate
  FROM weekly
)
SELECT industry, 
       week, 
       conv_rate, 
       prev_conv_rate,
       ROUND(conv_rate - COALESCE(prev_conv_rate, conv_rate), 2) AS wow_delta
FROM rates 
ORDER BY industry, week;

test_cases.label: Technology industry week-over-week conversion rates for Q1-Q2 2024
Finance industry shows week-over-week delta across multiple weeks
First week of each industry has NULL previous rate

metadata.reference_approach: Build a CTE that aggregates conversions and clicks by week and industry. Calculate the conversion rate (conversions/clicks * 100) per week. Apply LAG() window function partitioned by industry and ordered by week to retrieve the previous week's conversion rate. Finally, compute the week-over-week delta by subtracting the previous rate from the current rate. This approach ensures the rate is calculated first, then the LAG comparison is applied to the computed metric, avoiding issues with lagging raw numerators/denominators.

starter_code.sql:
-- Write your SQL query here
-- Calculate tip rate percentage broken down by vehicle type for completed rides

reference_solution.sql:
SELECT d.vehicle_type,
       COUNT(r.ride_id) AS total_rides,
       SUM(CASE WHEN r.tip > 0 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS tipped_rides,
       ROUND(100.0 * SUM(CASE WHEN r.tip > 0 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) / COUNT(r.ride_id), 2) AS tip_rate_pct,
       ROUND(AVG(CASE WHEN r.tip > 0 THEN r.tip END), 2) AS avg_tip_when_tipped
FROM rides r
JOIN drivers d ON r.driver_id = d.driver_id
WHERE r.status = 'completed'
GROUP BY d.vehicle_type
ORDER BY tip_rate_pct DESC;

test_cases.label: All vehicle types with tip percentages
Sedan vehicle type tip rate calculation
Edge case: Vehicle type with zero tips

metadata.reference_approach: Join the rides table to the drivers table on driver_id to access vehicle_type. Filter for completed rides using the WHERE clause. Group by vehicle_type and calculate: the total number of rides per vehicle type, the count of rides with tips using a CASE WHEN expression that returns 1 when tip > 0 and 0 otherwise, and the tip rate percentage by dividing tipped rides by total rides and multiplying by 100, rounded to 2 decimals. Additionally, compute the average tip amount only for rides where a tip was given by using CASE WHEN in the AVG function (NULL values from ELSE are automatically ignored). Order the results by tip_rate_pct in descending order to highlight vehicle types with the highest tipping rates.

starter_code.sql:
-- Write your SQL query here
-- Show week-over-week conversion rate change per industry for 2024
-- Use CTEs: first compute weekly rates, then apply LAG() window function

reference_solution.sql:
WITH weekly AS (
  SELECT 
    a.industry,
    DATE('2024-01-01', '+' || ((CAST((julianday(ap.date) - julianday('2024-01-01')) / 7 AS INTEGER)) * 7) || ' days') AS week,
    SUM(ap.conversions) AS convs,
    SUM(ap.clicks) AS clicks
  FROM ad_performance ap
  JOIN campaigns c ON ap.campaign_id = c.campaign_id
  JOIN advertisers a ON c.advertiser_id = a.advertiser_id
  WHERE ap.date >= '2024-01-01'
  GROUP BY a.industry, DATE('2024-01-01', '+' || ((CAST((julianday(ap.date) - julianday('2024-01-01')) / 7 AS INTEGER)) * 7) || ' days')
),
rates AS (
  SELECT 
    industry,
    week,
    ROUND(100.0 * convs / NULLIF(clicks, 0), 2) AS conv_rate,
    LAG(ROUND(100.0 * convs / NULLIF(clicks, 0), 2)) OVER (PARTITION BY industry ORDER BY week) AS prev_rate
  FROM weekly
)
SELECT 
  industry,
  week,
  conv_rate,
  prev_rate,
  ROUND(conv_rate - prev_rate, 2) AS wow_delta
FROM rates
ORDER BY industry, week;

test_cases.label: Basic WoW conversion rate change for Technology industry
All industries with complete week-over-week deltas
First week of each industry should have NULL previous rate

metadata.reference_approach: Create a CTE to compute weekly aggregated conversions and clicks per industry, then calculate conversion rates (conversions/clicks * 100) in that same CTE. In a second CTE, use the LAG() window function partitioned by industry and ordered by week to retrieve the previous week's conversion rate. Finally, in the main query, calculate the week-over-week delta by subtracting the previous rate from the current rate, ordering results by industry and week for clarity.

title: Google: Over-Budget Campaigns
problem_statement_markdown: What percentage of campaigns have spent more than their allocated budget?

starter_code.sql:
-- Write your SQL query here
-- Calculate the percentage of campaigns that have spent more than their allocated budget
-- Hint: Pre-aggregate ad_performance by campaign_id before joining to campaigns

reference_solution.sql: SELECT COUNT(*) AS total, SUM(CASE WHEN s.total_spend > c.budget THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS over_budget, ROUND(100.0 * SUM(CASE WHEN s.total_spend > c.budget THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) / COUNT(*), 2) AS over_budget_pct FROM campaigns c JOIN (SELECT campaign_id, SUM(spend) AS total_spend FROM ad_performance GROUP BY campaign_id) s ON c.campaign_id = s.campaign_id;

test_cases.label: Percentage of over-budget campaigns with mixed data
All output columns present and correctly calculated
Edge case: campaigns with exact budget match

metadata.reference_approach: Pre-aggregate ad_performance by campaign_id using a subquery to calculate total_spend per campaign, avoiding row fan-out. Join this aggregated result with campaigns table. Use CASE WHEN to identify campaigns where total_spend exceeds the allocated budget. Calculate the percentage by dividing the count of over-budget campaigns by total campaigns, multiplied by 100 and rounded to 2 decimal places.

title: Lyft: Tip Rate by Vehicle Type
problem_statement_markdown: What percentage of completed rides received a tip, broken down by vehicle type?

starter_code.sql:
-- Write your SQL query here
-- Find the percentage of completed rides that received a tip, grouped by vehicle type

reference_solution.sql: SELECT d.vehicle_type, COUNT(r.ride_id) AS rides, SUM(CASE WHEN r.tip>0 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS tipped, ROUND(100.0*SUM(CASE WHEN r.tip>0 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)/COUNT(r.ride_id),2) AS tip_rate_pct, ROUND(AVG(CASE WHEN r.tip>0 THEN r.tip END),2) AS avg_tip_when_tipped FROM rides r JOIN drivers d ON r.driver_id=d.driver_id WHERE r.status='completed' GROUP BY d.vehicle_type ORDER BY tip_rate_pct DESC;

test_cases.label: Basic tip rate by vehicle type with completed rides only
Verify average tip calculation for tipped rides
Edge case: vehicle type with all zero tips

metadata.reference_approach: Join the rides table to the drivers table on driver_id to access vehicle_type. Filter for completed rides only using WHERE r.status='completed'. Group results by vehicle_type. Use COUNT(r.ride_id) to count total rides per vehicle type. Use SUM(CASE WHEN r.tip>0 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) to count rides with tips. Calculate tip_rate_pct as the percentage of tipped rides. Use AVG(CASE WHEN r.tip>0 THEN r.tip END) to compute the average tip amount only for rides that received a tip (NULLs from implicit ELSE are ignored by AVG). Sort by tip_rate_pct in descending order to show highest tip rates first.

title: Google: High-CTR Campaigns
problem_statement_markdown: Which campaigns have a click-through rate (CTR = clicks/impressions) above 5%?

sql_schema.setup_script: CREATE TABLE advertisers (advertiser_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT, industry TEXT, country TEXT, budget_usd REAL); CREATE TABLE campaigns (campaign_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, advertiser_id INTEGER, name TEXT, start_date TEXT, end_date TEXT, budget REAL, status TEXT, FOREIGN KEY(advertiser_id) REFERENCES advertisers(advertiser_id)); starter_code.sql:
-- Write your SQL query here
-- Find campaigns with CTR (clicks/impressions) > 5%
-- Remember: CTR = clicks / impressions
-- Use NULLIF to avoid division by zero
-- Filter using HAVING, not WHERE
-- Cannot use SELECT aliases in HAVING, repeat the aggregate expression

reference_solution.sql: SELECT c.name, SUM(ap.impressions) AS impr, SUM(ap.clicks) AS clicks, ROUND(100.0 * SUM(ap.clicks) / NULLIF(SUM(ap.impressions), 0), 2) AS ctr_pct FROM campaigns c JOIN ad_performance ap ON c.campaign_id = ap.campaign_id GROUP BY c.campaign_id, c.name HAVING 100.0 * SUM(ap.clicks) / NULLIF(SUM(ap.impressions), 0) > 5 ORDER BY ctr_pct DESC;

metadata.reference_approach: Join the campaigns and ad_performance tables on campaign_id. Aggregate total impressions and clicks per campaign using SUM(). Calculate CTR as a percentage using 100.0 * SUM(clicks) / NULLIF(SUM(impressions), 0) to avoid division by zero. Use HAVING to filter for CTR > 5, repeating the full aggregate expression rather than referencing the SELECT alias. Order by CTR descending to show the best performers first.

-- Write your SQL query here
-- Find advertisers who ran active campaigns in both Q1 and Q2 of 2024

reference_solution.sql: SELECT advertiser_id FROM (SELECT DISTINCT c.advertiser_id FROM campaigns c WHERE c.start_date >= '2024-01-01' AND c.start_date < '2024-04-01') q1 INTERSECT SELECT advertiser_id FROM (SELECT DISTINCT c.advertiser_id FROM campaigns c WHERE c.start_date >= '2024-04-01' AND c.start_date < '2024-07-01') q2;

metadata.reference_approach: The solution uses the INTERSECT operator to find the overlap between two sets: (1) advertisers with campaigns starting in Q1 2024 (January 1 to March 31), and (2) advertisers with campaigns starting in Q2 2024 (April 1 to June 30). Each subquery selects DISTINCT advertiser_ids filtered by start_date ranges, and INTERSECT returns only those advertiser IDs present in both sets. This is the cleanest approach for 'present in both A and B' logic, avoiding self-joins or GROUP BY/HAVING complexity.

title: Lyft: High-Fare Cities
problem_statement_markdown: Which cities had an average completed ride fare above $25 in 2024?

starter_code.sql:
-- Write your SQL query here
-- Find cities with average completed ride fare above $25 in 2024

reference_solution.sql: SELECT city, ROUND(AVG(fare), 2) AS avg_fare, COUNT(*) AS ride_count FROM rides WHERE status = 'completed' AND pickup_time >= '2024-01-01' AND pickup_time < '2025-01-01' GROUP BY city HAVING AVG(fare) > 25 ORDER BY avg_fare DESC;

Chicago excluded with lower average fare

metadata.reference_approach: To solve this problem, filter the rides table to include only completed rides with pickup times in 2024. Then group by city and use the HAVING clause to filter for groups where the average fare exceeds $25. Calculate the average fare using AVG(fare) and count the number of rides per city. Round the average to 2 decimal places for clarity and sort the results by average fare in descending order to show the highest-earning cities first.

title: Lyft: Top 5 Earners per City
problem_statement_markdown: Who are the top 5 drivers by total earnings (fare + tip + incentives) in each city for 2024?

starter_code.sql:
-- Top 5 Earners per City for 2024
-- Use LEFT JOIN on incentives with COALESCE to preserve drivers with no incentives
-- Use RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY city) for per-city ranking
WRITE YOUR QUERY HERE

reference_solution.sql:
WITH earnings AS (
  SELECT 
    r.city,
    r.driver_id,
    d.name,
    SUM(r.fare + r.tip) AS ride_earn,
    COALESCE(SUM(i.amount), 0) AS incent_earn
  FROM rides r
  JOIN drivers d ON r.driver_id = d.driver_id
  LEFT JOIN incentives i ON r.driver_id = i.driver_id AND strftime('%Y', i.paid_at) = '2024'
  WHERE r.status = 'completed' AND strftime('%Y', r.pickup_time) = '2024'
  GROUP BY r.city, r.driver_id, d.name
),
ranked AS (
  SELECT 
    *,
    (ride_earn + incent_earn) AS total,
    RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY city ORDER BY (ride_earn + incent_earn) DESC) AS rnk
  FROM earnings
)
SELECT city, name, ride_earn, incent_earn, total, rnk
FROM ranked
WHERE rnk <= 5
ORDER BY city, rnk;

test_cases.label: Top 5 earners in San Francisco by total earnings
Top 5 earners in Los Angeles by total earnings
Driver with no incentives still appears in ranking

metadata.reference_approach: The solution uses a two-step CTE approach. First, it aggregates ride earnings (fare + tip) and incentives for each driver per city in 2024, using a LEFT JOIN on incentives to preserve drivers with no incentive records via COALESCE(SUM(), 0). Then it applies RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY city ORDER BY total DESC) to rank drivers within each city, finally filtering for rank 5 or better and sorting by city and rank for clear per-city breakdowns.

starter_code.sql:
-- Write your SQL query here
-- Show cumulative rides completed per driver, day by day, in January 2024
-- Use a CTE to aggregate daily rides, then apply SUM() OVER for running total

reference_solution.sql: WITH daily AS (SELECT driver_id, DATE(pickup_time) AS ride_date, COUNT(*) AS daily_rides FROM rides WHERE status='completed' AND pickup_time>='2024-01-01' AND pickup_time<'2024-02-01' GROUP BY driver_id, DATE(pickup_time)) SELECT driver_id, ride_date, daily_rides, SUM(daily_rides) OVER (PARTITION BY driver_id ORDER BY ride_date ROWS UNBOUNDED PRECEDING) AS cumulative_rides FROM daily ORDER BY driver_id, ride_date;

test_cases.label: Driver 1 cumulative rides across January days

Only completed rides counted, cancelled excluded

metadata.reference_approach: Solve this in two steps using a Common Table Expression (CTE). First, aggregate the completed rides in January by driver and date to get daily ride counts. Then, apply a window function SUM() OVER with PARTITION BY driver_id and ORDER BY ride_date, using ROWS UNBOUNDED PRECEDING to compute the cumulative sum of rides for each driver across all days they worked in January. This approach ensures you capture the running total progression day by day while filtering only completed rides in the specified month.

title: Shopify: Top 3 Categories per Plan
problem_statement_markdown: What are the top 3 product categories by revenue for each Shopify plan tier?

starter_code.sql:
-- Find the top 3 product categories by revenue for each Shopify plan tier
-- Use window functions to rank categories within each plan independently

reference_solution.sql:
WITH rev AS (
  SELECT 
    s.plan, 
    p.category, 
    SUM(o.total) AS cat_rev, 
    RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY s.plan ORDER BY SUM(o.total) DESC) AS rnk
  FROM stores s
  JOIN orders o ON s.store_id = o.store_id
  JOIN products p ON o.store_id = p.store_id
  WHERE o.status = 'completed'
  GROUP BY s.plan, p.category
)
SELECT plan, category, cat_rev, rnk 
FROM rev 
WHERE rnk <= 3 
ORDER BY plan, rnk;

test_cases.label: Basic plan top 3 categories by revenue
Premium plan top 3 categories by revenue

metadata.reference_approach: Aggregate revenue by plan and category using a CTE, calculating SUM(o.total) grouped by plan and category. Apply RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY plan ORDER BY total DESC) to independently rank categories within each plan tier. The PARTITION BY clause ensures rankings reset for each plan—without it, you'd get global rankings. Filter WHERE rnk <= 3 to keep only the top 3 per plan, then sort by plan and rank for clarity.

starter_code.sql:
-- Write your SQL query here
-- Calculate the percentage of completed orders from repeat customers
-- A repeat order is where order_date > first_order_date

reference_solution.sql:
SELECT 
  COUNT(*) AS total,
  SUM(CASE WHEN o.order_date > c.first_order_date THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS repeat_orders,
  ROUND(100.0 * SUM(CASE WHEN o.order_date > c.first_order_date THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) / COUNT(*), 2) AS repeat_rate_pct
FROM orders o
JOIN customers c ON o.customer_id = c.customer_id AND o.store_id = c.store_id
WHERE o.status = 'completed';

test_cases.label: Calculate repeat customer rate for completed orders
Verify total count and repeat count calculations
Edge case: orders on same day as first_order_date should not be repeat

metadata.reference_approach: Join the orders and customers tables on both customer_id and store_id to link each order with its customer's first_order_date. Filter for completed orders only. Use a CASE statement to identify repeat orders where the order_date is strictly greater than first_order_date. Count the total completed orders and sum the repeat orders, then calculate the percentage by dividing repeat orders by total orders, multiplying by 100, and rounding to 2 decimal places.

title: Shopify: Month-over-Month Store Revenue
problem_statement_markdown: Show month-over-month revenue growth rate per store for 2024.

starter_code.sql:
-- Write your SQL query here
-- Show month-over-month revenue growth rate per store for 2024
-- Use LAG() OVER (PARTITION BY store_id ORDER BY month) to calculate growth rate

reference_solution.sql:
WITH m AS (
  SELECT 
    store_id,
    DATE(strftime('%Y-%m-01', order_date)) AS month,
    SUM(total) AS revenue
  FROM orders
  WHERE status = 'completed' 
    AND order_date >= '2024-01-01' 
    AND order_date < '2025-01-01'
  GROUP BY store_id, DATE(strftime('%Y-%m-01', order_date))
)
SELECT 
  store_id,
  month,
  revenue,
  LAG(revenue) OVER (PARTITION BY store_id ORDER BY month) AS prev_revenue,
  ROUND(
    100.0 * (revenue - LAG(revenue) OVER (PARTITION BY store_id ORDER BY month)) / 
    NULLIF(LAG(revenue) OVER (PARTITION BY store_id ORDER BY month), 0),
    2
  ) AS mom_growth_pct
FROM m
ORDER BY store_id, month;

test_cases.label: Store with single month (NULL prev revenue)

metadata.reference_approach: Aggregate monthly revenue per store by grouping completed 2024 orders by store_id and month. Apply LAG() window function with PARTITION BY store_id and ORDER BY month to retrieve the prior month's revenue for each store. Calculate the growth percentage as (current_revenue - previous_revenue) / previous_revenue * 100, using NULLIF to avoid division by zero for the first month of each store. The PARTITION BY store_id is critical—without it, LAG would cross store boundaries and incorrectly compare revenue across different stores.

title: Shopify: Low Inventory Alert
problem_statement_markdown: Which products have sold more than 50 units total but have fewer than 10 units in inventory?

starter_code.sql:
-- Write your SQL query here
-- Find products with inventory < 10 AND estimated units sold > 50
-- Units sold estimated as: order_total / product_price

reference_solution.sql:
SELECT p.name, p.category, p.inventory, SUM(o.total / NULLIF(p.price, 0)) AS est_units_sold
FROM products p
JOIN orders o ON p.store_id = o.store_id
WHERE o.status = 'completed' AND p.inventory < 10
GROUP BY p.product_id, p.name, p.category, p.inventory, p.price
HAVING SUM(o.total / NULLIF(p.price, 0)) > 50
ORDER BY p.inventory;

test_cases.label: Products with low inventory and high sales
Edge case: product with zero price should be handled

metadata.reference_approach: This problem requires joining products with orders on store_id, filtering for completed orders and inventory below 10 in the WHERE clause. Estimate units sold by dividing order totals by product price (using NULLIF to avoid division by zero). Group by product details including inventory, then use HAVING to filter for products with estimated sales exceeding 50 units. The key insight is that inventory must be included in GROUP BY since it's a non-aggregated column we want to display and filter on.

title: DoorDash: Promo vs Non-Promo Order Value
problem_statement_markdown: Do orders with a promotional discount have higher or lower average subtotals than non-promo orders?

starter_code.sql:
-- Write your SQL query here
-- Compare average subtotals for orders with and without promotional discounts
-- Use a LEFT JOIN between orders and promotions to categorize them
SELECT
  -- Your query here
  ;

reference_solution.sql: SELECT CASE WHEN p.promo_id IS NOT NULL THEN 'Promo' ELSE 'Non-Promo' END AS order_type, COUNT(o.order_id) AS orders, ROUND(AVG(o.subtotal), 2) AS avg_subtotal, ROUND(AVG(o.delivery_fee), 2) AS avg_fee FROM orders o LEFT JOIN promotions p ON o.order_id = p.order_id WHERE o.status = 'delivered' GROUP BY CASE WHEN p.promo_id IS NOT NULL THEN 'Promo' ELSE 'Non-Promo' END;

test_cases.label: Compare promo vs non-promo average subtotals
Verify order counts match data (4 promo, 6 non-promo)
Edge case: Only delivered orders included

metadata.reference_approach: Use a LEFT JOIN between orders and promotions on order_id to preserve all orders while flagging those with applied promotions. Filter for delivered orders only. Classify each order as 'Promo' if promo_id is not NULL (indicating a promotion was applied) or 'Non-Promo' otherwise. Group by this classification and calculate aggregate statistics including order count, average subtotal, and average delivery fee. This single-pass approach efficiently compares metrics across both cohorts.

title: DoorDash: On-Time Delivery Rate
problem_statement_markdown: What percentage of orders were delivered within the restaurant's estimated delivery time?

starter_code.sql:
-- Write your SQL query here
-- Calculate the percentage of orders delivered within the restaurant's estimated delivery time
-- Compare actual delivery time (delivery_time - order_time) against each restaurant's avg_delivery_time_min
SELECT
  -- Your answer here

reference_solution.sql:
SELECT
  COUNT(*) AS total,
  SUM(CASE WHEN (julianday(o.delivery_time) - julianday(o.order_time)) * 24 * 60 <= r.avg_delivery_time_min THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS on_time,
  ROUND(100.0 * SUM(CASE WHEN (julianday(o.delivery_time) - julianday(o.order_time)) * 24 * 60 <= r.avg_delivery_time_min THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) / COUNT(*), 2) AS on_time_rate_pct
FROM orders o
JOIN restaurants r ON o.restaurant_id = r.restaurant_id
WHERE o.status = 'delivered';

metadata.reference_approach: Join orders with restaurants to access each restaurant's estimated delivery time threshold. For each delivered order, calculate the actual delivery duration in minutes by subtracting order_time from delivery_time using SQLite's julianday() function. Use a CASE WHEN statement to flag orders where actual duration is less than or equal to the restaurant's avg_delivery_time_min. Count total delivered orders and sum the on-time flagged orders, then calculate the percentage. Filter to only 'delivered' status orders since cancelled or pending orders should not be included in this metric.

title: DoorDash: Multi-Category Customers
problem_statement_markdown: Which customers ordered from 3 or more different restaurant categories in the last month?

starter_code.sql:
-- Write your SQL query here
-- Find customers who ordered from 3+ different restaurant categories in the last 30 days

reference_solution.sql: SELECT o.customer_id, COUNT(DISTINCT r.category) AS cats FROM orders o JOIN restaurants r ON o.restaurant_id = r.restaurant_id WHERE o.status = 'delivered' AND o.order_time >= datetime('now', '-30 days') GROUP BY o.customer_id HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT r.category) >= 3 ORDER BY cats DESC;

test_cases.label: Customer 1 (Alice) has 3 categories in last 30 days
Customer 2 (Bob) has 2 categories - should not appear

metadata.reference_approach: This problem requires aggregating order data at the customer level to identify behavioral breadth. The key insight is using COUNT(DISTINCT category) to count unique restaurant categories per customer. Filter for delivered orders within the 30-day window, then join with the restaurants table to access category information. Group by customer_id and apply a HAVING clause to retain only those with 3+ distinct categories. Finally, order by category count descending to show the most diverse customers first.

title: DoorDash: Top 3 Restaurants per City
problem_statement_markdown: What are the top 3 restaurants by total revenue in each city?

starter_code.sql:
-- Write your SQL query here
-- Find the top 3 restaurants by total revenue in each city
-- Only consider delivered orders
-- Return: city, restaurant name, total revenue, and rank

reference_solution.sql:
WITH rk AS (
  SELECT 
    r.city, 
    r.name, 
    SUM(o.subtotal) AS total_rev, 
    RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY r.city ORDER BY SUM(o.subtotal) DESC) AS rnk 
  FROM orders o 
  JOIN restaurants r ON o.restaurant_id = r.restaurant_id 
  WHERE o.status = 'delivered' 
  GROUP BY r.city, r.restaurant_id, r.name
) 
SELECT city, name, total_rev, rnk 
FROM rk 
WHERE rnk <= 3 
ORDER BY city, rnk;

test_cases.label: Top 3 restaurants in San Francisco by revenue
Top 3 restaurants in New York by revenue
Exclude cancelled orders and verify only delivered status counted

metadata.reference_approach: Aggregate the subtotal amount for each restaurant across all delivered orders using a GROUP BY on city, restaurant_id, and name. Use the RANK() window function partitioned by city and ordered by total revenue in descending order to rank restaurants within each city. Finally, filter for the top 3 ranks (rnk <= 3) and sort by city and rank for clear presentation. This approach ensures that ties in revenue are handled by RANK() which leaves gaps in ranking, making it suitable for this top-N problem.

title: GitHub: Top 5 Forked Repos per Language
problem_statement_markdown: What are the top 5 most forked public repositories per programming language?

starter_code.sql:
-- Write your SQL query here
-- Find the top 5 most forked public repositories per programming language

reference_solution.sql: WITH rk AS (SELECT language, name, forks, RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY language ORDER BY forks DESC) AS rnk FROM repos WHERE is_public=1 AND language IS NOT NULL) SELECT language, name, forks, rnk FROM rk WHERE rnk<=5 ORDER BY language, rnk;

test_cases.label: Top 5 forked repos per language - Python
Top 5 forked repos per language - JavaScript
Exclude private repos and NULL languages
Handle languages with fewer than 5 repos

metadata.reference_approach: Use a CTE with the RANK() window function partitioned by language and ordered by forks in descending order to rank each repository within its language group. Filter the original repos table to include only public repositories with non-NULL languages. Then in the outer query, select only those rows where the rank is 5 or less, effectively getting the top 5 forked repos per language. Finally, order the results by language and rank for clean presentation.

title: GitHub: Weekly Commit Activity Trend
problem_statement_markdown: For the top 10 repos by stars, show weekly commit count and 4-week rolling average for 2024.

starter_code.sql:
-- Write your SQL query here
-- Hint: Use a CTE to aggregate commits by week first,
-- then apply the 4-week rolling average window function

reference_solution.sql:
WITH top10 AS (
  SELECT repo_id 
  FROM repos 
  WHERE is_public = 1 
  ORDER BY stars DESC 
  LIMIT 10
),
weekly AS (
  SELECT 
    c.repo_id,
    DATE(c.committed_at, 'weekday 1', '-7 days') AS week,
    COUNT(*) AS commits
  FROM commits c
  JOIN top10 t ON c.repo_id = t.repo_id
  WHERE c.committed_at >= '2024-01-01' AND c.committed_at < '2025-01-01'
  GROUP BY c.repo_id, DATE(c.committed_at, 'weekday 1', '-7 days')
)
SELECT 
  repo_id,
  week,
  commits,
  ROUND(AVG(commits) OVER (PARTITION BY repo_id ORDER BY week ROWS BETWEEN 3 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW), 1) AS rolling_4w
FROM weekly
ORDER BY repo_id, week;

test_cases.label: Top 10 repos weekly commits for 2024 with 4-week rolling average
Verify rolling average calculation for repo 1 (highest stars)
Edge case: repo with single week of commits

metadata.reference_approach: The solution identifies the top 10 public repositories by star count, then aggregates commit counts into weekly buckets using a CTE. A window function computes a 4-week rolling average (including the current week and the 3 preceding weeks) partitioned by repository and ordered chronologically. This approach avoids mixing raw table rows with window functions by pre-aggregating commits into weekly summaries first, ensuring the rolling window operates on properly grouped data.

title: Slack: WAU Sender Rate by Plan
problem_statement_markdown: What percentage of users sent at least one message this week, by workspace plan?

starter_code.sql:
-- Write your SQL query here
-- Calculate the percentage of users who sent at least one message this week, grouped by workspace plan

reference_solution.sql: SELECT w.plan, COUNT(DISTINCT u.user_id) AS total_users, COUNT(DISTINCT m.user_id) AS senders, ROUND(100.0*COUNT(DISTINCT m.user_id)/COUNT(DISTINCT u.user_id),2) AS sender_rate_pct FROM workspaces w JOIN users u ON w.workspace_id=u.workspace_id LEFT JOIN messages m ON u.user_id=m.user_id AND m.sent_at>=date('now','-7 days') GROUP BY w.plan ORDER BY sender_rate_pct DESC;

test_cases.label: Calculate WAU sender rate for all plans
Pro plan should have higher sender rate than standard

metadata.reference_approach: Join workspaces with users to get all users per plan, then LEFT JOIN messages with a date condition (last 7 days) on the right side. The LEFT JOIN ensures all users appear in results even if they sent no messages. COUNT(DISTINCT m.user_id) counts only users who sent messages (NULLs are skipped), while COUNT(DISTINCT u.user_id) counts all users. Divide message senders by total users and multiply by 100 to get the percentage, rounded to 2 decimal places. Group by plan and order by sender rate descending.

title: Slack: Power User Channel Dominance
problem_statement_markdown: Find channels where the top 3 users account for more than 80% of all messages.

sql_schema.setup_script:

starter_code.sql:
-- Find channels where the top 3 users account for more than 80% of all messages
-- Use three CTEs: totals, user rankings, and top 3 aggregation

WITH totals AS (
  -- CTE 1: Calculate total messages per channel
  SELECT channel_id, COUNT(*) AS total
  FROM messages
  GROUP BY channel_id
),
urk AS (
  -- CTE 2: Rank users by message count within each channel
  SELECT channel_id, user_id, COUNT(*) AS cnt,
         RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY channel_id ORDER BY COUNT(*) DESC) AS rnk
  FROM messages
  GROUP BY channel_id, user_id
),
top3 AS (
  -- CTE 3: Sum top 3 users' messages and join with totals
  SELECT u.channel_id, SUM(u.cnt) AS top3_msgs, t.total
  FROM urk u
  JOIN totals t ON u.channel_id = t.channel_id
  WHERE u.rnk <= 3
  GROUP BY u.channel_id, t.total
)
SELECT channel_id, top3_msgs, total, 
       ROUND(100.0 * top3_msgs / total, 2) AS top3_pct
FROM top3
WHERE 100.0 * top3_msgs / total > 80
ORDER BY top3_pct DESC;

reference_solution.sql:
WITH totals AS (
  SELECT channel_id, COUNT(*) AS total
  FROM messages
  GROUP BY channel_id
),
urk AS (
  SELECT channel_id, user_id, COUNT(*) AS cnt,
         RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY channel_id ORDER BY COUNT(*) DESC) AS rnk
  FROM messages
  GROUP BY channel_id, user_id
),
top3 AS (
  SELECT u.channel_id, SUM(u.cnt) AS top3_msgs, t.total
  FROM urk u
  JOIN totals t ON u.channel_id = t.channel_id
  WHERE u.rnk <= 3
  GROUP BY u.channel_id, t.total
)
SELECT channel_id, top3_msgs, total, ROUND(100.0 * top3_msgs / total, 2) AS top3_pct
FROM top3
WHERE 100.0 * top3_msgs / total > 80
ORDER BY top3_pct DESC;

test_cases.label: Channel 101 (general) where top 3 users exceed 80% threshold
Edge case: channels below 80% threshold excluded

metadata.reference_approach: The solution uses three cascading CTEs to identify power user channels. First, compute total message counts per channel. Second, rank users within each channel by message frequency using a window function. Third, aggregate the top 3 ranked users' message counts per channel and compute their percentage share of total messages. Finally, filter for channels where this share exceeds 80% and sort by dominance descending. This approach isolates channels with extreme message concentration among a small user base.

title: Instacart: Top 5 Products per Store
problem_statement_markdown: What are the top 5 most ordered products (by unique buyers) per store?

starter_code.sql:
-- Write your SQL query here
-- Find the top 5 most ordered products (by unique buyers) per store
-- Rank by COUNT(DISTINCT user_id) to avoid bulk buyers inflating rankings

reference_solution.sql: WITH rk AS (SELECT p.store_id, p.product_id, p.name, COUNT(DISTINCT o.user_id) AS unique_buyers, RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY p.store_id ORDER BY COUNT(DISTINCT o.user_id) DESC) AS rnk FROM order_items oi JOIN products p ON oi.product_id = p.product_id JOIN orders o ON oi.order_id = o.order_id WHERE o.status = 'delivered' GROUP BY p.store_id, p.product_id, p.name) SELECT store_id, product_id, name, unique_buyers, rnk FROM rk WHERE rnk <= 5 ORDER BY store_id, rnk;

test_cases.label: Top 5 products by unique buyers for store 1
Top 5 products by unique buyers for store 2
Results ordered by store_id and rank

metadata.reference_approach: Use a CTE with window function RANK() to count unique buyers (DISTINCT user_id) per product per store, filtering only delivered orders. Partition by store_id and order by unique buyer count descending to get per-store rankings. Filter for rank <= 5 to get top 5 per store, then order final results by store_id and rank for clarity.

title: Instacart: Delivery Time by Hour
problem_statement_markdown: What is the average delivery time (in minutes) by hour of day? Which hours are slowest?

starter_code.sql:
-- Write your SQL query here
-- Calculate average delivery time (in minutes) by hour of day
-- Identify which hours are slowest

reference_solution.sql: SELECT CAST(STRFTIME('%H', order_time) AS INTEGER) AS order_hour, COUNT(*) AS order_count, ROUND(AVG((JULIANDAY(delivery_time) - JULIANDAY(order_time)) * 24 * 60), 1) AS avg_min FROM orders WHERE status = 'delivered' AND delivery_time IS NOT NULL GROUP BY CAST(STRFTIME('%H', order_time) AS INTEGER) ORDER BY avg_min DESC;

test_cases.label: All delivered orders by hour with average delivery time
Slowest hours ranked by average delivery time descending
Exclude pending and null delivery times

metadata.reference_approach: Extract the hour from the order_time using STRFTIME('%H', order_time), then calculate the delivery duration in minutes by computing the difference between delivery_time and order_time (using JULIANDAY which returns fractional days, multiply by 24*60 to convert to minutes). Filter for only delivered orders with non-null delivery times, group by hour, calculate the average delivery time per hour, and sort in descending order to identify the slowest hours.

title: HubSpot: Top 5 Sales Reps by Revenue

starter_code.sql:

test_cases.label: Top 5 sales reps by closed-won revenue

metadata.reference_approach: Aggregate deal amounts by owner_id for closed_won deals using GROUP BY and SUM(). Then apply the RANK() window function to rank sales reps by their total revenue in descending order. Finally, order by rank and limit to the top 5. This approach ensures we first compute aggregates per rep, then rank those aggregated values, avoiding the common mistake of trying to apply ranking before aggregation.

title: HubSpot: Contacts by Lifecycle Stage
problem_statement_markdown: Which lifecycle stages have more than 500 contacts?

reference_solution.sql: SELECT lifecycle_stage, COUNT(*) AS contact_count FROM contacts GROUP BY lifecycle_stage HAVING COUNT(*) > 500 ORDER BY contact_count DESC;

test_cases.label: Result includes contact_count column in descending order

metadata.reference_approach: This problem uses GROUP BY to aggregate contacts by lifecycle_stage, then applies a HAVING clause to filter groups where the count exceeds 500. The HAVING clause operates on aggregated data (unlike WHERE which filters rows before aggregation), making it essential for filtering groups based on aggregate functions. The result is ordered by contact_count in descending order to show the largest groups first.

title: Snowflake: Failed Query Rate
problem_statement_markdown: What percentage of queries failed, broken down by account plan?

starter_code.sql:
-- Calculate the percentage of failed queries broken down by account plan
-- Use CASE WHEN to count failures in a single pass
SELECT
  -- plan,
  -- total_queries,
  -- failed,
  -- fail_rate_pct
FROM
  queries q
  JOIN accounts a ON q.account_id = a.account_id
WHERE
  1=1
GROUP BY
  a.plan
ORDER BY
  fail_rate_pct DESC;

reference_solution.sql: SELECT a.plan, COUNT(q.query_id) AS total_queries, SUM(CASE WHEN q.status='failed' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS failed, ROUND(100.0*SUM(CASE WHEN q.status='failed' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)/COUNT(q.query_id),2) AS fail_rate_pct FROM queries q JOIN accounts a ON q.account_id=a.account_id GROUP BY a.plan ORDER BY fail_rate_pct DESC;

metadata.reference_approach: This problem demonstrates the efficiency of using a single CASE WHEN expression within aggregate functions to calculate percentages in one pass. Rather than running separate COUNT(*) queries filtered for successes and failures, we use SUM(CASE WHEN status='failed' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) to count failures and COUNT(query_id) for the total in the same GROUP BY operation. This avoids scanning the queries table multiple times and ensures consistent grouping. The percentage is calculated as (failed/total)*100, rounded to 2 decimal places, and results are ordered by failure rate descending to highlight problem areas by plan type.

title: HubSpot: Contacts Who Never Clicked Despite Multiple Emails
problem_statement_markdown: Which contacts received emails from 3 or more campaigns but never clicked any?

starter_code.sql:
-- Find contacts who received emails from 3+ campaigns but never clicked any
-- Use HAVING to filter on multiple aggregate conditions
SELECT
  -- your columns here
FROM emails e
WHERE e.bounced = 0
GROUP BY e.contact_id
HAVING
  -- aggregate conditions here
ORDER BY campaigns_received DESC;

reference_solution.sql: SELECT e.contact_id, COUNT(DISTINCT e.campaign_id) AS campaigns_received FROM emails e WHERE e.bounced = 0 GROUP BY e.contact_id HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT e.campaign_id) >= 3 AND SUM(CASE WHEN e.clicked_at IS NOT NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) = 0 ORDER BY campaigns_received DESC;

test_cases.label: Contact with clicks excluded from result

metadata.reference_approach: This query identifies engaged-but-unresponsive contacts by combining two distinct aggregate calculations in the HAVING clause. First, COUNT(DISTINCT campaign_id) ensures we only get contacts who received emails from at least 3 different campaigns. Second, SUM(CASE WHEN clicked_at IS NOT NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) = 0 ensures that none of those emails were clicked. The WHERE clause filters out bounced emails to focus on successfully delivered messages. GROUP BY contact_id aggregates all emails per contact, and ORDER BY campaigns_received DESC prioritizes contacts who received the most emails without clicking.

starter_code.sql:
-- Write your SQL query here
-- Calculate weekly lead-to-opportunity conversion rate with 4-week rolling average

reference_solution.sql:
WITH weekly AS (
  SELECT 
    DATE(created_at, 'weekday 0') AS week,
    SUM(CASE WHEN lifecycle_stage='lead' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS leads,
    SUM(CASE WHEN lifecycle_stage='opportunity' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS opportunities
  FROM contacts
  WHERE created_at >= DATE('2024-11-09', '-3 months')
  GROUP BY DATE(created_at, 'weekday 0')
)
SELECT 
  week,
  leads,
  opportunities,
  ROUND(100.0 * opportunities / NULLIF(leads, 0), 2) AS conv_rate,
  ROUND(AVG(100.0 * opportunities / NULLIF(leads, 0)) OVER (
    ORDER BY week 
    ROWS BETWEEN 3 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW
  ), 2) AS rolling_4w_avg
FROM weekly
ORDER BY week;

test_cases.label: Weekly conversion rates with 4-week rolling average
Verify rolling average calculation over 4 weeks
Edge case: weeks with zero leads (division by zero handling)

metadata.reference_approach: First, use a CTE to group contacts by week and count leads vs opportunities for the last 3 months. Then calculate the weekly conversion rate as a percentage (opportunities/leads). Finally, apply a window function with AVG() and a 4-week rolling window (ROWS BETWEEN 3 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) to compute the rolling average of conversion rates, not raw counts. Use NULLIF to handle division by zero.

title: HubSpot: Email Open and Click Rates
problem_statement_markdown: What is the email open rate and click rate for each campaign?

starter_code.sql:
-- Write your SQL query here
-- Calculate email open and click rates for each campaign
-- Remember: exclude bounced emails from the calculation
SELECT
  c.name,
  COUNT(e.email_id) AS sent,
  SUM(CASE WHEN e.opened_at IS NOT NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS opened,
  SUM(CASE WHEN e.clicked_at IS NOT NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS clicked,
  ROUND(100.0 * SUM(CASE WHEN e.opened_at IS NOT NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) / NULLIF(COUNT(e.email_id), 0), 2) AS open_rate_pct,
  ROUND(100.0 * SUM(CASE WHEN e.clicked_at IS NOT NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) / NULLIF(COUNT(e.email_id), 0), 2) AS click_rate_pct
FROM campaigns c
JOIN emails e ON c.campaign_id = e.campaign_id
WHERE e.bounced = 0
GROUP BY c.campaign_id, c.name
ORDER BY open_rate_pct DESC;

reference_solution.sql: SELECT c.name, COUNT(e.email_id) AS sent, SUM(CASE WHEN e.opened_at IS NOT NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS opened, SUM(CASE WHEN e.clicked_at IS NOT NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS clicked, ROUND(100.0 * SUM(CASE WHEN e.opened_at IS NOT NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) / NULLIF(COUNT(e.email_id), 0), 2) AS open_rate_pct, ROUND(100.0 * SUM(CASE WHEN e.clicked_at IS NOT NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) / NULLIF(COUNT(e.email_id), 0), 2) AS click_rate_pct FROM campaigns c JOIN emails e ON c.campaign_id = e.campaign_id WHERE e.bounced = 0 GROUP BY c.campaign_id, c.name ORDER BY open_rate_pct DESC;

test_cases.label: All campaigns ordered by open rate
Campaign with no opens or clicks

metadata.reference_approach: Filter out bounced emails first using WHERE e.bounced = 0, since bounces represent undelivered messages that shouldn't count toward engagement metrics. Then aggregate email engagement using CASE WHEN to count opens and clicks, calculate rates as percentages using 100.0 * count / total, and use NULLIF to prevent division by zero errors. Group by campaign and order by open rate to show performance rankings.

title: Snowflake: Top 5 Expensive Queries per Account
problem_statement_markdown: What are the top 5 most credit-intensive queries per account?

starter_code.sql:
-- Write your SQL query here
-- Hint: Use RANK() window function partitioned by account_id
-- Filter for successful queries only
-- Return top 5 per account ordered by credits_used descending

reference_solution.sql:
WITH rk AS (
  SELECT account_id, query_id, credits_used, duration_sec, bytes_scanned,
         RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY account_id ORDER BY credits_used DESC) AS rnk
  FROM queries
  WHERE status='success'
)
SELECT account_id, query_id, credits_used, duration_sec, bytes_scanned, rnk
FROM rk
WHERE rnk <= 5
ORDER BY account_id, rnk;

test_cases.label: Account 2 top 5 expensive queries (filters failed status)
Account 3 returns fewer than 5 rows (only 3 successful queries)
Ranking is correct within each account partition

metadata.reference_approach: Use a window function RANK() partitioned by account_id and ordered by credits_used descending to rank queries within each account. Filter the queries table to only include successful executions before ranking to avoid counting failed queries. Then filter the ranked results to keep only the top 5 (rnk <= 5) per account. This avoids pre-aggregation and ranks individual query rows directly, capturing all relevant metrics (duration, bytes scanned) alongside the credit cost.
