title: Two Sum
problem_statement_markdown: Given an array of integers nums and an integer target, return the indices of the two numbers that add up to target. You may assume exactly one solution exists, and you may not use the same element twice.

metadata.reference_approach: Use a hash map to store each number's index as you iterate. For each element, compute the complement (target - num) and check if it already exists in the map. If it does, return [map[complement], current_index]. This gives O(n) time and O(n) space, compared to the O(n²) brute-force nested-loop approach.

title: Word Break
problem_statement_markdown: Given a string and a dictionary of words, determine if the string can be segmented into a space-separated sequence of one or more dictionary words.

metadata.reference_approach: Build a boolean DP array dp of length n+1 where dp[i] means s[:i] can be segmented using words from the dictionary. Initialize dp[0]=True (empty string). For each end index i from 1 to n, try every split point j from 0 to i-1: if dp[j] is True and s[j:i] is in the word set, set dp[i]=True and break. Return dp[n]. Time O(n^2), space O(n).

title: Validate Binary Search Tree
problem_statement_markdown: Given a binary tree, determine if it is a valid binary search tree (BST). Each node's value must be strictly greater than all values in its left subtree and strictly less than all values in its right subtree. This constraint must hold for every node, not just immediate children.

test_cases.label: subtree violation [10,5,15,null,null,6,20] - 6 in right subtree but 6 < 10

metadata.reference_approach: Pass min/max bounds during DFS traversal. The naive approach of checking left.val < root.val < right.val fails for subtree violations (e.g. a node deep in the right subtree that is less than the root). Instead, each recursive call carries a valid range [min_val, max_val] and rejects any node whose value falls outside it. The bounds tighten as we go deeper: going left sets a new upper bound (node.val), going right sets a new lower bound. Time O(n), space O(h).

title: Design LFU Cache
problem_statement_markdown:
Implement a Least Frequently Used (LFU) Cache with O(1) get and put operations. The cache has a fixed capacity. When eviction is needed, remove the key with the lowest access frequency. If multiple keys have the same minimum frequency, evict the least recently used among them.

The solution function receives two arrays: operations (strings) and args (arrays of arguments). Return an array of results — use null for void operations (constructor, put) and the integer result for get.

test_cases.label: update existing key value without eviction
tie in min-freq evicts LRU: get(1) raises freq, put(3) evicts key 2 (LRU at min-freq)

metadata.reference_approach: Use two hash maps and a min_freq tracker for O(1) all operations. key_map maps each key to its [value, freq]. freq_map maps each frequency to an OrderedDict (Python) or insertion-order Map (JS) of keys at that frequency — the insertion order gives LRU semantics within a frequency bucket. On get: update frequency, shift key from freq_map[f] to freq_map[f+1]; if freq_map[f] empties and f == min_freq, increment min_freq. On put with eviction: pop the leftmost (least recently used) key from freq_map[min_freq], then insert the new key at freq 1 and reset min_freq to 1. Updating an existing key also calls _update but does not reset min_freq. Time O(1) per op, space O(capacity).

title: Car Fleet
problem_statement_markdown: N cars head to the same destination. Given positions and speeds, cars that catch up form a fleet. Return number of fleets that arrive.

test_cases.label: all cars caught by slowest front car

metadata.reference_approach: Sort cars by starting position in descending order (closest to target first). Compute the time each car needs to reach the target. Use a monotonic stack: iterate through cars from closest to farthest. If the current car's time-to-destination is greater than the top of the stack, it forms a new fleet (it cannot catch up to the car ahead). Otherwise, it joins the fleet of the car ahead (stack top remains). The final stack size is the number of fleets. Time O(n log n) due to sorting, Space O(n) for the stack.

title: Coin Change
problem_statement_markdown: Given coins of different denominations and a target amount, find the minimum number of coins needed to make up that amount. Return -1 if it is impossible to make up the amount.

metadata.reference_approach: Use bottom-up dynamic programming where dp[i] represents the minimum number of coins needed to make amount i. Initialize dp[0] = 0 (base case) and all other values to infinity. For each amount from 1 to the target, iterate over all coin denominations and update dp[i] = min(dp[i], dp[i - c] + 1) whenever coin c <= i. The answer is dp[amount] if it's not infinity, otherwise -1. Time complexity: O(amount * len(coins)), Space complexity: O(amount).

title: Container With Most Water
problem_statement_markdown: Given n non-negative integers representing heights of vertical lines, find two lines that together with the x-axis form a container that holds the most water.

test_cases.label: Two tall spikes at ends of interior

metadata.reference_approach: Use two pointers starting at both ends of the array. At each step, compute the area as min(h[left], h[right]) * (right - left) and update the maximum. Move the pointer pointing to the shorter line inward, since keeping the taller line gives the best chance of finding a larger area with a smaller width. This greedy strategy is provably optimal. Time complexity: O(n), Space complexity: O(1).

title: House Robber
problem_statement_markdown: Rob houses in a row; you cannot rob two adjacent houses. Find the maximum amount of money you can rob.

test_cases.label: nine-house array with large middle value

metadata.reference_approach: Use dynamic programming with two variables tracking the best outcome up to the previous house (prev1) and two houses back (prev2). For each house, the new best is max(skip current = prev1, rob current = prev2 + nums[i]). Update both variables in a single pass. Time O(n), Space O(1).

title: House Robber II (Circular)
problem_statement_markdown: Houses are arranged in a circle, meaning the first and last houses are adjacent. Find the maximum amount of money you can rob without robbing adjacent houses.

metadata.reference_approach: Reduce the circular house robber problem to two instances of the linear House Robber I problem. Since the first and last houses are adjacent, in any optimal solution we cannot include both. So we solve two subproblems: rob houses 0..n-2 (exclude last) and rob houses 1..n-1 (exclude first), then take the maximum. Each subproblem is solved with a simple DP using two variables tracking the best rob amount up to the current and previous house. Time complexity O(n), Space complexity O(1).

title: Largest Rectangle in Histogram
problem_statement_markdown: Given an array of bar heights in a histogram, find the largest rectangle that can be formed.

reference_solution.python:
def solution(heights):
    stack = []  # monotonic increasing
    max_area = 0
    heights = heights + [0]  # sentinel, don't mutate input
    for i, h in enumerate(heights):
        start = i
        while stack and stack[-1][1] >= h:
            idx, height = stack.pop()
            max_area = max(max_area, height * (i - idx))
            start = idx
        stack.append((start, h))
    return max_area

metadata.reference_approach: Use a monotonic increasing stack storing (start_index, height) pairs. Iterate through the heights with a sentinel 0 appended at the end. For each bar, if it is shorter than the stack top, pop bars and compute their rectangle area using the current index as the right boundary and the popped bar's stored start as the left boundary. Track the earliest start index that can extend to the current position. This runs in O(n) time and O(n) space since each bar is pushed and popped at most once.

title: Generate Parentheses
problem_statement_markdown: Generate all combinations of n pairs of well-formed (valid) parentheses.

metadata.reference_approach: Use backtracking with two counters tracking open and close parentheses used so far. At each step, add '(' if open_count < n (we still have opening parens to place), and add ')' if close_count < open_count (we can close a previously opened paren). When the string reaches length 2*n, it's a valid combination. This generates all valid combinations without ever producing invalid ones. Time complexity is O(4^n / sqrt(n)) — the nth Catalan number — and space complexity is O(n) for the recursion stack.

title: Minimum Cost to Connect Ropes
problem_statement_markdown: Given rope lengths, connect all ropes into one. Cost = sum of two ropes being connected. Minimize total cost.

reference_solution.python:
import heapq

def solution(ropes):
    if not ropes:
        return 0
    if len(ropes) == 1:
        return 0
    heap = ropes[:]
    heapq.heapify(heap)
    total_cost = 0
    
    while len(heap) > 1:
        a = heapq.heappop(heap)
        b = heapq.heappop(heap)
        cost = a + b
        total_cost += cost
        heapq.heappush(heap, cost)
    
    return total_cost

metadata.reference_approach: Use a min-heap (priority queue) to always combine the two shortest ropes first. Pop the two smallest values, add them together to get the connection cost, accumulate that cost into the total, then push the combined rope back into the heap. Repeat until only one rope remains. This greedy approach (equivalent to Huffman coding) ensures the minimum total cost because shorter ropes are combined earlier and thus contribute less to subsequent sums. Time complexity: O(n log n) for n heap operations; Space complexity: O(n) for the heap.

title: Minimum Window Substring
problem_statement_markdown: Given strings s and t, return the minimum window substring of s that contains all characters of t. If none exists, return empty string.

starter_code.python:
def solution(s: str, t: str) -> str:
    pass

reference_solution.python:
def solution(s: str, t: str) -> str:
    if not t or not s:
        return ""
    
    from collections import Counter
    
    need = Counter(t)
    have = {}
    
    have_count = 0
    need_count = len(need)
    
    left = 0
    min_len = float('inf')
    min_left = 0
    
    for right in range(len(s)):
        char = s[right]
        have[char] = have.get(char, 0) + 1
        
        if char in need and have[char] == need[char]:
            have_count += 1
        
        while have_count == need_count:
            # Update minimum window
            window_len = right - left + 1
            if window_len < min_len:
                min_len = window_len
                min_left = left
            
            # Shrink from left
            left_char = s[left]
            have[left_char] -= 1
            if left_char in need and have[left_char] < need[left_char]:
                have_count -= 1
            left += 1
    
    if min_len == float('inf'):
        return ""
    return s[min_left:min_left + min_len]

metadata.reference_approach: Use a sliding window with two frequency maps (need for t, have for current window). Expand the right pointer adding characters to the window. Track have_count (number of unique chars in t satisfied by window). When have_count equals need_count (all chars covered), record the window if it's the smallest so far, then shrink from the left by incrementing left pointer and updating have counts. This runs in O(|s| + |t|) time since each character is added and removed at most once, and O(|s| + |t|) space for the frequency maps.

title: Reorganize String
problem_statement_markdown: Rearrange characters so no two adjacent characters are the same. Return empty string if impossible.

metadata.reference_approach: Use a max-heap (by frequency) of (negative_count, character) pairs. Repeatedly pop the two most frequent characters, append both to the result, decrement their counts, and re-push if count > 0. If only one character remains in the heap with count > 1, return empty string (impossible). Otherwise append the last character. Time O(n log 26) = O(n), Space O(n).

title: Sum of Two Integers (Without + or -)
problem_statement_markdown: Calculate the sum of two integers a and b without using + or - operators.

starter_code.python:
def solution(a: int, b: int) -> int:
    pass

metadata.reference_approach: Use bitwise XOR to compute the sum without carry, and bitwise AND shifted left by 1 to compute the carry. Repeat until carry is zero. A 32-bit mask (0xFFFFFFFF) is applied at each step to handle Python's arbitrary-precision integers — after the loop, check the sign bit (bit 31) of a; if set the result is negative and we sign-extend with `a | ~mask`. Time complexity is O(1) since integers are bounded to 32 bits; space complexity is O(1).

title: Top K Frequent Elements
problem_statement_markdown: Find the k most frequent elements in an array. Result can be in any order.

metadata.reference_approach: Use Python's Counter to tally element frequencies in O(n), then use heapq.nlargest to extract the top k keys by frequency in O(n log k). This combines to O(n log k) time and O(n) space. An alternative is bucket sort: create buckets indexed by frequency (1 to n), place each element in its bucket, then scan from highest to lowest frequency collecting k elements in O(n) time.

title: Inorder Traversal (Iterative)
problem_statement_markdown: Implement binary tree inorder traversal iteratively (without recursion).

reference_solution.python:
def solution(root):
    result = []
    stack = []
    current = root

    while stack or current:
        while current:
            stack.append(current)
            current = current.left
        current = stack.pop()
        result.append(current.val)
        current = current.right

    return result

metadata.reference_approach: Use an explicit stack to simulate the call stack of recursive inorder traversal. Maintain a 'current' pointer starting at root. In a loop, push all left-spine nodes onto the stack (going as far left as possible), then pop a node, record its value, and move current to its right child. Repeat until both stack and current are empty. This visits nodes in left-root-right order. Time complexity is O(n) since each node is pushed and popped exactly once. Space complexity is O(h) where h is the tree height (O(log n) balanced, O(n) skewed).

title: Invert Binary Tree
problem_statement_markdown: Invert (mirror) a binary tree. Every left child becomes right child and vice versa.

metadata.reference_approach: Use a recursive post-order traversal: for each node, recursively invert the left and right subtrees, then swap them. Base case is a null node. This visits every node exactly once giving O(n) time complexity and O(h) space complexity where h is the tree height (call stack depth), which is O(log n) for balanced trees and O(n) worst case for skewed trees.

title: Serialize and Deserialize Binary Tree
problem_statement_markdown: Design algorithms to serialize a binary tree to a string and deserialize it back. Your serialization format is your choice.

reference_solution.python:
from collections import deque

def solution(root):
    # Serialize
    tokens = []
    def dfs(node):
        if not node:
            tokens.append('null')
            return
        tokens.append(str(node.val))
        dfs(node.left)
        dfs(node.right)
    dfs(root)
    data = ','.join(tokens)
    
    # Deserialize
    queue = deque(data.split(','))
    def build():
        val = queue.popleft()
        if val == 'null':
            return None
        node = TreeNode(int(val))
        node.left = build()
        node.right = build()
        return node
    return build()

test_cases.label: tree with null in middle level

metadata.reference_approach: Use pre-order DFS (NLR) traversal to serialize: visit each node and append its value, or 'null' for missing children, joined by commas. To deserialize, split the string into a token queue and recursively consume tokens — if the token is 'null' return None, otherwise create a TreeNode and recursively build its left then right subtrees. Both operations run in O(n) time and O(n) space where n is the number of nodes.

title: N-Queens
problem_statement_markdown: Place n queens on an n x n chessboard so no two queens attack each other. Return all distinct board solutions.

reference_solution.python:
def solution(n):
    result = []
    cols = set()
    diag1 = set()  # row - col
    diag2 = set()  # row + col
    board = [['.' ] * n for _ in range(n)]

    def backtrack(row):
        if row == n:
            result.append([''.join(r) for r in board])
            return
        for col in range(n):
            if col in cols or (row - col) in diag1 or (row + col) in diag2:
                continue
            board[row][col] = 'Q'
            cols.add(col)
            diag1.add(row - col)
            diag2.add(row + col)
            backtrack(row + 1)
            board[row][col] = '.'
            cols.remove(col)
            diag1.remove(row - col)
            diag2.remove(row + col)

    backtrack(0)
    return result

metadata.reference_approach: Backtrack row by row, placing exactly one queen per row. Track occupied columns and both diagonals using sets: the column index, the (row - col) value for the anti-diagonal, and the (row + col) value for the main diagonal. For each row try every column; if no conflict exists on any of the three sets, place the queen, recurse to the next row, then remove it. When all n rows are filled, record the board state. This yields all valid placements in O(n!) time with O(n) space for the recursion stack and the three sets.

title: Sudoku Solver
problem_statement_markdown: Solve a 9x9 Sudoku puzzle in-place. Fill '.' cells with digits 1-9 satisfying row, column, and 3x3 box constraints.

metadata.reference_approach: Scan the board for the first empty cell ('.'). Try placing each digit '1' through '9' in that cell, validating against the row, column, and 3x3 box using a single loop of nine checks. If placement is valid, recurse; if recursion succeeds, return the board. If no digit works, reset the cell to '.' and return False, triggering backtracking. When no empty cell remains, the board is fully solved. The solution returns the modified board for harness compatibility. Time complexity is O(9^m) where m is the number of empty cells; practical performance is much faster due to early pruning.

title: Merge K Sorted Lists
problem_statement_markdown: Merge k sorted lists into one sorted list. Each input list is a sorted array of integers.

starter_code.python:
import heapq
from typing import List

def solution(lists: List[List[int]]) -> List[int]:
    pass

metadata.reference_approach: Use a min-heap seeded with all (value, list_index, element_index) tuples from every input list. Repeatedly pop the minimum, append it to the result. This runs in O(n log n) time where n is the total number of elements. Alternatively, flatten all lists into one array and sort: O(n log n) time with simpler code. Both approaches avoid the linked-list harness issue by accepting plain 2D arrays as input.

title: Design Hit Counter (Last N Seconds)
problem_statement_markdown: Design a hit counter that counts the number of hits in the last 300 seconds. Implement two methods: hit(timestamp) — records a hit at the given timestamp (in seconds); getHits(timestamp) — returns the number of hits in the past 300 seconds from the given timestamp. Timestamps are monotonically increasing. Multiple hits can occur at the same timestamp.

test_cases.label: circular buffer eviction — same bucket reused overwrites stale timestamp

metadata.reference_approach: Use a circular buffer of size 300: two arrays 'times' and 'hits' indexed by timestamp % 300. For hit(t): if times[t%300] == t, increment hits[t%300]; else reset times[t%300]=t and hits[t%300]=1. For getHits(t): sum hits[i] for all i where t - times[i] < 300 (i.e., within the 300-second window). Time: O(1) for hit, O(300)=O(1) for getHits; Space: O(300)=O(1).

title: Design Search Autocomplete (Trie)
problem_statement_markdown: Design a search autocomplete system. Users type a character at a time and the system returns the top 3 most frequent historical sentences matching the current prefix. Implement: input(c) — input one character (# means end of sentence), returns top 3 suggestions by frequency (ties broken lexicographically).

test_cases.label: frequency accumulates after retyping — top order shifts on second query

metadata.reference_approach: Build a Trie where each node stores a frequency map of all complete sentences passing through it. On initialization, insert all historical sentences with their frequencies by traversing each character and updating freq_map at every node along the path. On input(c): if c is '#', insert the accumulated input as a new sentence (freq +1) and reset state; otherwise, walk one step down the trie. If the node exists, return the top-3 sentences from its freq_map sorted by (-frequency, lexicographic). If no node exists, return []. Time: O(m) per character for trie traversal + O(k log k) for top-3 extraction where k=distinct sentences; Space: O(N*M) where N=sentences, M=avg length.

title: Kth Largest Element in Stream
problem_statement_markdown: Design a class that finds the kth largest element in a stream. Constructor takes k and initial array; add(val) returns current kth largest.

test_cases.label: k equals heap size — adding larger value bumps kth up

metadata.reference_approach: Maintain a min-heap of size k. During initialization and each add operation, push the new element and if the heap exceeds size k, pop the minimum. The heap root (minimum of the k largest elements) is always the kth largest. Time complexity: O(n log k) for initialization, O(log k) per add operation. Space complexity: O(k).

title: Pacific Atlantic Water Flow
problem_statement_markdown: Given a height matrix, find all cells that can flow to both the Pacific Ocean (top/left) and Atlantic Ocean (bottom/right). Water flows to equal-height or lower cells.

reference_solution.python:
from collections import deque

def solution(heights):
    if not heights:
        return []
    rows, cols = len(heights), len(heights[0])
    directions = [(1,0),(-1,0),(0,1),(0,-1)]

    def bfs(starts):
        visited = set(starts)
        queue = deque(starts)
        while queue:
            r, c = queue.popleft()
            for dr, dc in directions:
                nr, nc = r + dr, c + dc
                if (0 <= nr < rows and 0 <= nc < cols
                        and (nr, nc) not in visited
                        and heights[nr][nc] >= heights[r][c]):
                    visited.add((nr, nc))
                    queue.append((nr, nc))
        return visited

    pacific_starts = [(0, c) for c in range(cols)] + [(r, 0) for r in range(rows)]
    atlantic_starts = [(rows-1, c) for c in range(cols)] + [(r, cols-1) for r in range(rows)]

    pacific = bfs(pacific_starts)
    atlantic = bfs(atlantic_starts)

    return [list(cell) for cell in pacific & atlantic]

test_cases.label: Strictly decreasing grid — only Pacific-border cells reach both oceans

metadata.reference_approach: Perform reverse BFS from each ocean's border cells (Pacific: top row + left column; Atlantic: bottom row + right column), climbing uphill (visiting neighbors with height >= current). This finds all cells that can reach each ocean. The answer is the intersection of both reachable sets. Time: O(m*n) since each cell is visited at most twice. Space: O(m*n) for the visited sets and BFS queues.

title: Smallest Range Covering K Lists
problem_statement_markdown: Given k sorted lists, find the smallest range [a,b] that includes at least one element from each list.

reference_solution.python:
import heapq

def solution(nums):
    heap = [(lst[0], i, 0) for i, lst in enumerate(nums)]
    heapq.heapify(heap)
    cur_max = max(lst[0] for lst in nums)
    best = [heap[0][0], cur_max]
    
    while True:
        cur_min, i, j = heapq.heappop(heap)
        if j + 1 >= len(nums[i]):
            break
        next_val = nums[i][j + 1]
        heapq.heappush(heap, (next_val, i, j + 1))
        cur_max = max(cur_max, next_val)
        cur_min = heap[0][0]
        if cur_max - cur_min < best[1] - best[0]:
            best = [cur_min, cur_max]
    
    return best

test_cases.label: three interleaved lists — initial window is already optimal

metadata.reference_approach: Use a min-heap initialized with the first element from each of the k lists, tracking the current maximum value across all heap entries. At each step, extract the minimum element, compute the current range [min, max], and update the best answer if the range is smaller. Then advance that list's pointer and push the next element into the heap, updating the global max. Stop when any list is exhausted (no valid range can cover all lists from that point). Time complexity O(n log k) where n is total elements, space O(k) for the heap.

title: Word Ladder
problem_statement_markdown: Given beginWord, endWord, and wordList, find the length of the shortest transformation sequence where each step changes exactly one letter and each intermediate word must be in wordList.

test_cases.label: no path exists despite endWord in list — intermediate cluster disconnected

metadata.reference_approach: BFS from beginWord. If endWord is not in the word set, return 0 immediately. Use a queue of (word, length) pairs and a visited set. For each word dequeued, try all 26-letter substitutions at each position. If a substitution equals endWord, return length+1. If it is in the word set and unvisited, enqueue it with length+1 and mark visited. Return 0 if the queue empties without finding endWord. Time O(M^2 * N) where M is word length and N is wordList size.

title: Word Search II (Trie + Backtracking)
problem_statement_markdown: Given an m×n board of characters and a list of words, return all words from the list that can be found in the board. A word is formed by sequentially adjacent cells (horizontally or vertically) and each cell may not be reused within a single word path.

test_cases.label: standard board - finds oath and eat
word longer than any board path
same word not counted twice when appears via different paths

metadata.reference_approach: Build a Trie from the word list so all words share common prefixes, enabling early pruning during DFS. For each cell on the board, start a DFS that follows trie edges — if the current character is not in the trie node, prune immediately. Mark cells visited with a sentinel character during recursion and restore on backtrack. Store the complete word at trie leaf nodes and delete after finding to prevent duplicates. Time complexity is O(M * 4 * 3^(L-1)) per word where M is board cells and L is word length; the Trie amortizes prefix work across all words. Space O(sum of word lengths) for the trie.

title: LinkedIn: Most Connected Users
problem_statement_markdown: Who are the top 10 most connected users on the platform?

test_cases.label: Edge: user with no connections excluded

starter_code.sql: -- Write your SQL query here

reference_solution.sql:
SELECT user_id, COUNT(*) AS connection_count
FROM (
  SELECT user_id_1 AS user_id FROM connections
  UNION ALL
  SELECT user_id_2 AS user_id FROM connections
) all_connections
GROUP BY user_id
ORDER BY connection_count DESC
LIMIT 10;

metadata.reference_approach: Union all connections from both user_id_1 and user_id_2 columns to get every user's connection appearances. Group by user_id and count rows. Order descending and limit to 10. Using UNION ALL (not UNION) is critical — UNION would deduplicate rows that happen to share the same user_id value across both columns.

test_cases.label: WoW change per track this week
LAG window function applied per track
Edge: track with no prior week streams shows NULL

starter_code.sql: -- Write your SQL query here

reference_solution.sql:
WITH weekly AS (
  SELECT track_id,
         strftime('%Y-%W', streamed_at) AS week,
         COUNT(*) AS stream_count
  FROM streams
  WHERE streamed_at >= date('now', '-14 days')
  GROUP BY track_id, strftime('%Y-%W', streamed_at)
),
with_prev AS (
  SELECT track_id, week, stream_count,
         LAG(stream_count) OVER (PARTITION BY track_id ORDER BY week) AS prev_week_streams
  FROM weekly
)
SELECT w.track_id, t.title, w.stream_count,
       w.prev_week_streams,
       ROUND(100.0 * (w.stream_count - w.prev_week_streams) / NULLIF(w.prev_week_streams, 0), 2) AS wow_change_pct
FROM with_prev w JOIN tracks t ON w.track_id = t.track_id
WHERE w.week = strftime('%Y-%W', 'now')
ORDER BY wow_change_pct DESC;

metadata.reference_approach: CTE 'weekly' aggregates stream counts per track per ISO week using strftime('%Y-%W') in SQLite. CTE 'with_prev' applies LAG(1) partitioned by track_id to fetch the prior week's count. Outer query filters to the current week and computes percentage change using NULLIF to avoid division by zero.

test_cases.label: Drivers with 100+ rides and zero tips in 2024

starter_code.sql: -- Write your SQL query here

reference_solution.sql:
SELECT d.driver_id, d.name, COUNT(r.ride_id) AS rides_completed
FROM drivers d
JOIN rides r ON d.driver_id = r.driver_id
WHERE r.status = 'completed'
  AND r.pickup_time >= '2024-01-01'
  AND r.pickup_time < '2025-01-01'
GROUP BY d.driver_id, d.name
HAVING COUNT(r.ride_id) > 100 AND SUM(r.tip) = 0
ORDER BY rides_completed DESC;

metadata.reference_approach: Join drivers to rides filtering for completed status and pickup in calendar year 2024. Group by driver. HAVING applies two conditions: COUNT > 100 ensures high volume, and SUM(tip) = 0 ensures no tips across all those rides. Ordering by ride count descending.

starter_code.sql: -- Write your SQL query here

reference_solution.sql:
SELECT p.department, SUM(oi.quantity) AS total_units
FROM order_items oi
JOIN products p ON oi.product_id = p.product_id
JOIN orders o ON oi.order_id = o.order_id
WHERE o.order_time >= date('now', '-7 days')
  AND o.status = 'delivered'
GROUP BY p.department
HAVING SUM(oi.quantity) > 10000
ORDER BY total_units DESC;

metadata.reference_approach: Join order_items to products (for department) and to orders (for date and status filtering). Filter delivered orders from the last 7 days. Group by department. HAVING filters on SUM(quantity) — not COUNT(*) — because quantity tracks units ordered, not row count. Order descending.

test_cases.label: Stores with 100+ completed orders in first 30 days
Per-store date window in JOIN not static WHERE

starter_code.sql: -- Write your SQL query here

reference_solution.sql:
SELECT s.store_id, s.name, s.plan, COUNT(o.order_id) AS first_month_orders
FROM stores s
JOIN orders o
  ON s.store_id = o.store_id
  AND o.order_date BETWEEN s.created_at AND date(s.created_at, '+30 days')
WHERE o.status = 'completed'
GROUP BY s.store_id, s.name, s.plan
HAVING COUNT(o.order_id) > 100
ORDER BY first_month_orders DESC;

metadata.reference_approach: The 30-day window is per-store and relative to each store's created_at, so it belongs in the JOIN ON clause as a BETWEEN condition. A static WHERE date filter would not work correctly since each store launched at a different time. Filter completed status in WHERE. Group by store and use HAVING to keep only stores exceeding 100 orders.

INSERT INTO pull_requests
SELECT 300+n, 4, (n%10)+1, (n%5)+1, '2024-04-' || printf('%02d',1+(n%28)),
  CASE WHEN n<=10 THEN '2024-04-' || printf('%02d',3+(n%28)) ELSE NULL END,
  CASE WHEN n<=10 THEN 'merged' ELSE 'closed' END
FROM (WITH RECURSIVE c(n) AS (SELECT 1 UNION ALL SELECT n+1 FROM c WHERE n<25) SELECT n FROM c);

-- Repo 5: 5 PRs, 5 merged = 100% but fails count threshold
INSERT INTO pull_requests
SELECT 400+n, 5, (n%10)+1, (n%5)+1, '2024-05-' || printf('%02d',1+(n%28)),
  '2024-05-' || printf('%02d',3+(n%28)),
  'merged'
FROM (WITH RECURSIVE c(n) AS (SELECT 1 UNION ALL SELECT n+1 FROM c WHERE n<5) SELECT n FROM c);

-- Repo 6: 18 PRs, 11 merged = 61.1% (fails rate)
INSERT INTO pull_requests
SELECT 500+n, 6, (n%10)+1, (n%5)+1, '2024-06-' || printf('%02d',1+(n%28)),
  CASE WHEN n<=11 THEN '2024-06-' || printf('%02d',3+(n%28)) ELSE NULL END,
  CASE WHEN n<=11 THEN 'merged' ELSE 'open' END
FROM (WITH RECURSIVE c(n) AS (SELECT 1 UNION ALL SELECT n+1 FROM c WHERE n<18) SELECT n FROM c);

-- Repo 7: 40 PRs, 30 merged = 75% (passes)
INSERT INTO pull_requests
SELECT 600+n, 7, (n%10)+1, (n%5)+1, '2024-07-' || printf('%02d',1+(n%28)),
  CASE WHEN n<=30 THEN '2024-07-' || printf('%02d',3+(n%28)) ELSE NULL END,

FROM (WITH RECURSIVE c(n) AS (SELECT 1 UNION ALL SELECT n+1 FROM c WHERE n<40) SELECT n FROM c);

test_cases.label: Repos with merge rate above 70% and at least 10 PRs
CASE WHEN used to compute merged count
Edge: small repo with 100% rate but <10 PRs excluded

starter_code.sql: -- Write your SQL query here

reference_solution.sql:
SELECT repo_id,
       COUNT(*) AS total_prs,
       SUM(CASE WHEN status = 'merged' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS merged,
       ROUND(100.0 * SUM(CASE WHEN status = 'merged' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) / COUNT(*), 2) AS merge_rate_pct
FROM pull_requests
GROUP BY repo_id
HAVING COUNT(*) >= 10
  AND 100.0 * SUM(CASE WHEN status = 'merged' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) / COUNT(*) > 70
ORDER BY merge_rate_pct DESC;

metadata.reference_approach: Group pull_requests by repo_id. Use CASE WHEN inside SUM to count only merged PRs. Compute merge rate as 100.0 * merged / total. HAVING applies two conditions: at least 10 PRs (minimum sample size to prevent misleading 100% from a single merged PR) and rate above 70. Order by merge rate descending.

Edge: workspace with inactive majority excluded

starter_code.sql: -- Write your SQL query here

reference_solution.sql:
SELECT w.workspace_id, w.name, w.plan, COUNT(u.user_id) AS active_users
FROM workspaces w
JOIN users u ON w.workspace_id = u.workspace_id

ORDER BY active_users DESC;

metadata.reference_approach: Filter users with last_active_at within the last 7 days in the WHERE clause — this is a row-level filter and belongs in WHERE, not HAVING. Join to workspaces for plan and name. Group by workspace and use HAVING COUNT > 100 to keep only the most engaged workspaces.

title: Slack: DAU by Plan with Rolling Average
problem_statement_markdown: Show DAU (users who sent at least one message) by plan over the last 30 days, with 7-day rolling average.

sql_schema.setup_script:
CREATE TABLE workspaces (workspace_id INTEGER, name TEXT, plan TEXT, created_at TEXT, employee_count INTEGER);
CREATE TABLE messages (message_id INTEGER, workspace_id INTEGER, channel_id INTEGER, user_id INTEGER, sent_at TEXT, message_type TEXT, reactions_count INTEGER);

INSERT INTO workspaces VALUES
(1,'Acme Corp','enterprise','2020-01-10',5000),
(2,'Startup Hub','pro','2021-06-01',120),
(3,'BigBank','enterprise','2019-03-15',12000),
(4,'DevShop','pro','2022-02-20',80),
(5,'MediaCo','business','2020-11-05',3500),
(6,'LocalBiz','business','2021-08-15',50),
(7,'TechTeam','free','2023-01-01',45),
(8,'SmallStart','free','2023-06-01',20);

-- Generate 30 days of messages: enterprise, pro, business, free plans
-- enterprise: workspaces 1 and 3, ~80 distinct users per day
-- pro: workspaces 2 and 4, ~25 distinct users per day
-- business: workspaces 5 and 6, ~15 distinct users per day
-- free: workspaces 7 and 8, ~8 distinct users per day

INSERT INTO messages
SELECT
  ROW_NUMBER() OVER () AS message_id,
  ws.workspace_id,
  1 AS channel_id,
  ws.user_id,
  date('now', '-' || day_offset || ' days') AS sent_at,
  'text' AS message_type,
  0 AS reactions_count
FROM (
  WITH RECURSIVE days(d) AS (SELECT 0 UNION ALL SELECT d+1 FROM days WHERE d < 29)
  SELECT d AS day_offset FROM days
) AS date_range
CROSS JOIN (
  -- enterprise users
  SELECT 1 AS workspace_id, ((n-1) % 80) + 1 AS user_id FROM (WITH RECURSIVE c(n) AS (SELECT 1 UNION ALL SELECT n+1 FROM c WHERE n<90) SELECT n FROM c)
  UNION ALL
  SELECT 3 AS workspace_id, 100 + ((n-1) % 80) + 1 AS user_id FROM (WITH RECURSIVE c(n) AS (SELECT 1 UNION ALL SELECT n+1 FROM c WHERE n<90) SELECT n FROM c)
  UNION ALL
  -- pro users
  SELECT 2 AS workspace_id, 300 + ((n-1) % 25) + 1 AS user_id FROM (WITH RECURSIVE c(n) AS (SELECT 1 UNION ALL SELECT n+1 FROM c WHERE n<30) SELECT n FROM c)
  UNION ALL
  SELECT 4 AS workspace_id, 400 + ((n-1) % 25) + 1 AS user_id FROM (WITH RECURSIVE c(n) AS (SELECT 1 UNION ALL SELECT n+1 FROM c WHERE n<30) SELECT n FROM c)
  UNION ALL
  -- business users
  SELECT 5 AS workspace_id, 500 + ((n-1) % 12) + 1 AS user_id FROM (WITH RECURSIVE c(n) AS (SELECT 1 UNION ALL SELECT n+1 FROM c WHERE n<15) SELECT n FROM c)
  UNION ALL
  SELECT 6 AS workspace_id, 600 + ((n-1) % 8) + 1 AS user_id FROM (WITH RECURSIVE c(n) AS (SELECT 1 UNION ALL SELECT n+1 FROM c WHERE n<10) SELECT n FROM c)
  UNION ALL
  -- free users
  SELECT 7 AS workspace_id, 700 + ((n-1) % 5) + 1 AS user_id FROM (WITH RECURSIVE c(n) AS (SELECT 1 UNION ALL SELECT n+1 FROM c WHERE n<7) SELECT n FROM c)
  UNION ALL
  SELECT 8 AS workspace_id, 800 + ((n-1) % 4) + 1 AS user_id FROM (WITH RECURSIVE c(n) AS (SELECT 1 UNION ALL SELECT n+1 FROM c WHERE n<5) SELECT n FROM c)
) AS ws;

test_cases.label: DAU per plan per day with 7-day rolling average
Window partitioned by plan keeps plans separate
Edge: first 6 days have partial window (fewer than 7 days)

starter_code.sql: -- Write your SQL query here

reference_solution.sql:
WITH daily AS (
  SELECT w.plan,
         date(m.sent_at) AS day,
         COUNT(DISTINCT m.user_id) AS dau
  FROM messages m
  JOIN workspaces w ON m.workspace_id = w.workspace_id
  WHERE m.sent_at >= date('now', '-30 days')
  GROUP BY w.plan, date(m.sent_at)
)
SELECT plan, day, dau,
       ROUND(AVG(dau) OVER (
         PARTITION BY plan
         ORDER BY day
         ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW
       ), 0) AS rolling_7d
FROM daily
ORDER BY plan, day;

metadata.reference_approach: CTE 'daily' computes distinct active users per plan per day by joining messages to workspaces and filtering to the last 30 days. The outer query applies a window function AVG partitioned by plan ordered by day with a 6-PRECEDING frame (7 rows including current). PARTITION BY plan prevents the rolling window from crossing plan boundaries. SQLite supports this window syntax natively.
